data.title: TfL failed to spot dozens of ‘potential bombs’ at stations
data.description:
Transport for London (TfL) staff failed more than a third of tests designed to see whether they could spot potential bombs left at railway stations, The Telegraph can disclose.

Unattended items placed by undercover inspectors were missed on 22 out of 63 occasions in the past year, according to an internal bulletin sent earlier this month.

The bulletin was sent on the same day that MI5’s Joint Terrorism Analysis Centre raised the terrorist threat level to “severe”, meaning a terrorist attack in the UK was “highly likely”, following the Golders Green stabbings in April.

The Conservatives called for a review of TfL’s security protocols and for the data to be published in full.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said: “Londoners are entitled to know whether the people responsible for keeping them safe are taking that responsibility seriously...

“TfL are failing the basic security standards Londoners have every right to expect on their transport network. Sadiq Khan has treated TfL as a vanity project for a decade, racking up huge debts on the service, so no wonder issues like this arise when resources are stretched.”

Railway stations are subject to unannounced spot checks by Department for Transport (DfT) inspectors under security rules.

Staff must regularly inspect areas such as platforms and cupboards containing firefighting equipment – and even look inside hollow yellow cones put out by cleaners to mark liquid spillages.

Security tests ‘difficult to pass’

The bulletin sent to TfL staff on May 1, seen by The Telegraph, said: “Now consider that we failed 22 of the 63 covert tests that DfT conducted during 2025/26 and all of those were failed because the person conducting the security check did not see the unattended item...

“The person tasked with checking all the public areas of the station, to a standard sufficient to spot an unattended item, failed in this relatively straightforward task on more than a third of occasions.”

A TfL spokesman said: “Safety and security are at the absolute centre of everything we do, and we work closely with the DfT, the police and other security partners to keep our network safe for our customers.

“The DfT conducts covert tests by leaving unattended items at a small number of stations to assess staff response. The tests are deliberately challenging and difficult to pass.

“This data, alongside a wide range of other insights and analysis, drives our comprehensive security improvement programme and we regularly hold ourselves to account for performance and continuous improvement.”

A DfT spokesman said: “Keeping passengers safe and secure is our top priority. Rail operators, including Transport for London, are required to meet strict security standards, and we work closely with them to ensure appropriate measures are in place across the network.

“We expect operators to take action where issues are identified, and we have enforcement powers where serious or repeated problems are found.”

In recent years, TfL has trialled AI software to monitor CCTV cameras at Willesden Green station, in north-west London, to detect safety and security incidents.

In the year-long trial, which ended in September 2023, the technology sought to detect passengers who displayed “aggressive behaviour” or brandished weapons, Wired magazine reported.

Sir Sadiq Khan’s spokesman did not respond to a request for comment on the security test.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why Mango founder’s ‘money obsessed’ heir is prime suspect in death fall
data.description:
When Isak Andic, the founder of Mango, died at the age of 71 after falling from a cliff while hiking in Spain, it was presumed to be a tragic accident.

The fashion mogul, worth an estimated $4.5bn (£3.4bn), had been walking in Montserrat natural park in Catalonia with his son Jonathan before slipping off the mountainside, his fashion company said.

Turkish-born billionaire’s family and business mourned his death.

“Let us continue to honour his memory and legacy, keeping alive the values and teachings he left us,” his son urged Mango employees at a ceremony in his memory. “May his example inspire us to be better every day and to continue.”

The accidental death narrative prevailed for months, but last week Catalan police said Jonathan Andic, 45, had visited the area of the fall three times before his father’s death on Dec 14, 2024.

They claimed they had seven key pieces of evidence, gathered during a one-and-a-half-year investigation, and said he was their chief suspect.

Raquel Nieto Galván, the judge in the case, ordered the Mango heir’s detention on May 19, writing that there was “sufficient evidence to suggest that the death may not have been accidental, and that [Jonathan Andic] played an active and premeditated role in his father’s death”.

Pictures of Mr Andic in handcuffs were splashed across Spanish newspapers, shocking many who had long seen his fashion company as a story of Catalan business success. He maintains his innocence, and his lawyer has said the “homicide theory does not hold up”.

He was later released on bail for €1m (about £860,000), forced to give up his passport and ordered to report to court every week. The judge will now decide whether to charge him or close the case.

In addition to the visits to the site, police evidence is said to include a missing mobile phone, an “improbable slip”, a change in Isak Andic’s will and a poor relationship between father and son.

Investigators also said they had identified inconsistencies in their suspect’s statements to police. He told police he had visited the site only once in the two weeks before his father’s death, yet tracking of his car showed it had been there on Dec 7, Dec 8 and Dec 10, 2024.

He told police his father had stopped to take photographs at the spot where he fell, but when ⁠police searched his body, his phone was in his pocket and had only been used for photographs at the beginning of the hike, according to a writ.

Mr Andic had called emergency services, and said he had been walking ahead of his father when he heard rocks sliding and turned before seeing that he had fallen. However, investigators said the footmarks from Isak Andic’s last steps did not match those of someone who had lost their footing.

Police said the position of the body and his injuries were inconsistent with an accidental fall. It appeared “as if he had launched himself down a slide, feet first”, and there were no injuries to his palms, ruling out the likelihood that he might have tripped on a rock.

Mr Andic told the judge he had a good relationship with his father, who was born in Istanbul, and denied any disagreements – professional or personal – between them.

However, police said witnesses had told them about a crisis in 2015, when Isak Andic handed the reins of his company to his son, only to retake control when Mango began to falter.

Mr Andic was described by the judge as someone with an “obsession with money”. According to the judge’s order, he had requested an inheritance from his father “while he was still alive”.

In WhatsApp messages, disclosed after Judge Galván asked for analysis of the suspect’s phone, he expressed “feelings of hatred, resentment and thoughts of death, blaming his father for his situation”.

Mr Andic – who maintains his innocence – confirmed to the judge that his father had withdrawn some of the power he had been given at Mango after the business declined. However, he denied that this had created any ill feeling.

Witnesses told Bloomberg the son was not to blame for Mango’s change in fortunes, and that he was surrounded by people who wanted to watch him fail. Nevertheless, his father turned to Toni Ruiz, his finance chief, who became chief executive in 2020.

The judge said Mr Andic had discovered in mid-2024 that his father had planned to change his will to create a foundation to help people in need – provoking a “marked change” in his son. He suffered a “professional, personal and family crisis, especially in relation to his father”, court documents said.

It is also alleged by the police that Jonathan Andic’s behaviour changed after he learned the case was being reopened. They allege he claimed his phone had been stolen and all its data lost during a three-day trip to Quito, Ecuador, in March last year.

His wife, Paula Nata, made her social media profiles private online following his arrest. According to reports in the Spanish press, she had angered Isak Andic by appearing in a photoshoot for Hola magazine without wearing Mango clothes.

An Andic family statement said “there does not exist, nor will there exist, legitimate evidence against [Jonathan]”. They criticised the case, saying it “stigmatises an innocent man”, adding: “The homicide theory is inconsistent, but above all it is painful ... the truth and his innocence will shine through.”

In 2013, an El País profile of Jonathan Andic described him as a rising star in the business world.

It said: “Undoubtedly, it is an imposing position for a young man of just 32 years old, but no one can escape the fact that, in eight years of experience, he has not taken a single false step. So, when you hear about it (and you will), don’t say we didn’t warn you.”

The Telegraph has approached his legal representatives and Mango for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Spurned Murdoch son plots own journalism empire
data.description:
Rupert Murdoch’s spurned younger son is plotting his own journalism empire after striking a deal to acquire New York Magazine.

James Murdoch has acquired the Vox news-and-opinion website, Vox Media’s podcast network and New York Magazine, which was once owned by his father.

Terms of the deal were not disclosed, but it is reportedly valued at more than $300m (£224m).

It marks the first major deal by the junior Murdoch son since a dramatic succession battle that concluded last year. The saga ended with a $3.3bn deal that handed control of the family’s media empire to Lachlan, James’s elder brother.

Lachlan is considered the most conservative of Rupert Murdoch’s children, meaning the deal will preserve the Right-wing stance of outlets including The Sun, Fox News and The Wall Street Journal.

James and his two other siblings – Elisabeth Murdoch and Prudence MacLeod – have more liberal politics than their father. Each received $1.1bn for their shares in the family trust.

James’s takeover of Vox signals a return to the fray for the 53-year-old, who previously led Sky and 21st Century Fox.

The media executive had widely been viewed as the heir apparent to the Murdoch media empire until he was forced to resign in the wake of the News of the World phone hacking scandal in 2012.

The saga fractured his relationship with his father and in the ensuing years he repeatedly clashed with the patriarch because of the media empire’s political direction.

James resigned from the board of News Corp in 2020 and took aim at the company’s outlets for their “ongoing denial” of climate change. He has also been an open critic of Fox News and said US media outlets “propagating lies” were in part to blame for the storming of the Capitol in 2021.

The younger son’s troubled relationship with his father was also laid bare during a high-profile Nevada trial over the future of the family trust last year. A lawyer representing Rupert and Lachlan Murdoch referred to James and his siblings as “white, privileged, multi-billionaire trust-fund babies”, with Rupert later revealed to have been texting the questions to the lawyer from across the room.

In a subsequent interview with the Atlantic, James described his father as a “misogynist” and said his behaviour was “f-----g twisted”.

His choice of acquisition underlines how his political viewpoint has shifted away from that of his father.

New York Magazine has a staunchly Left-wing political agenda and has been a vocal critic of Donald Trump’s administration.

While Vox.com shuns an overt leaning, describing itself as an “explanatory” outlet, its coverage of the Gaza conflict focusses significantly on the humanitarian crisis for Palestinians and has drawn accusations of anti-Israel bias from groups including Honest Reporting.

James is buying the Vox Media divisions through his company Lupa Systems. He denied that his father’s previous ownership of New York Magazine held any significance for him or that he was deliberately trying to build a media business that contrasted with his father’s.

He told the New York Times: “I’m just trying to build a great business.”

The deal will usher in a new era for Vox Media, which was one of a string of online media outlets that cropped up in the early digital age but have since struggled to hold their ground.

Rival Vice Media has collapsed into bankruptcy while BuzzFeed recently sold a 52pc stake for $120m – a fraction of its peak valuation of $1.7bn in 2016.

The Vox titles will operate as subsidiaries of Lupa Systems, which also holds stakes in the companies behind Art Basel and the Tribeca Film Festival. The new Vox Media will be led by Jim Bankoff, its co-founder.

The deal does not include other Vox Media titles, such as Eater, The Dodo and The Verge.

James said: “This acquisition aligns well with our existing holdings and investments and reflects both our interest in the forward edge of culture and our deep commitment to ambitious journalism and agenda-setting conversations.”

data.title: Heathrow faces nine-year wait to connect to grid as it plots expansion
data.description:
Heathrow is facing a nine-year wait to connect directly to the National Grid despite warnings that it will need more electrical power for its proposed third runway.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, said Heathrow had been told it could take until 2035 for a direct link to the National Grid to be put in place. It requested the link after a substation fire and power outage triggered the grounding of flights at the airport in March last year.

However, it follows years where Heathrow has been under pressure to boost its power supplies.

Even as it stands, Europe’s busiest airport needs twice the electrical power required a decade ago as it seeks to decarbonise its energy consumption.

With the third runway, part of a £49bn expansion plan that would lift passenger numbers from 85 million a year to as high as 140 million, it could need four times as much.

Mr Woldbye said Heathrow had originally been told to expect to wait 18 years for a grid connection and enlarged transfer station. He said even a revised offer of a nine-year wait was “not satisfactory”, saying a “clear prioritisation” was needed.

Mr Woldbye added: “Lots of companies put in speculative projects and say they want a place in the queue. At least as an airport we have everything ready and we’re not going to go away.”

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero is conducting a review into how best to implement recommendations for improving energy resilience at critical infrastructure made by the National Energy System Operator following the Heathrow fire.

Heathrow could still take legal action over the fire, but is awaiting the outcome of an Ofgem investigation into whether National Grid Electricity Transmission complied with legislation and licence conditions relating to its electricity system at North Hyde substation.

The warning over the wait for a National Grid connection came as Mr Woldbye raised concerns that the electrical power needed to fuel its runway project could be drained by 85-plus data centres built in the M4 corridor in recent years.

The data centre cluster, the largest in Europe and the second largest in the world, houses hundreds of thousands of computers, servers and storage systems.

Dozens more are in the pipeline, with the expansion already putting substantial strain on the local grid, leading to restrictions for new commercial and housing developments.

Mr Woldbye said: “You have lots of data centres popping up, particularly in west London.

“We would argue that a third runway is more critical national infrastructure than data centres, which conceivably you can put somewhere else. We can’t do with the airport.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: China takes over Europe’s decaying car factories
data.description:
Politicians and car industry bosses have long feared that the rising Chinese giants will flood Europe with cheap electric vehicles EV.

Brussels and Beijing even teetered on the edge of a trade war because of the matter last year.

But times have changed. With Europe’s automotive titans unable to sell enough vehicles to run their huge assembly lines profitably, they are inviting the Chinese in through the front door.

European car makers are starting to sell space on their half-unused factory floor to their Chinese rivals.

The likes of BYD, Geely, Dongfeng, Chery and Leapmotor can use these deals to reposition themselves as local producers, skirting EU tariffs and “Made in Europe” rules.

Whole factories may yet fall into Chinese hands, and the Europeans will even help the Chinese sell the product. It is a case of “if you can’t beat them, join them”.

“This is effectively the beginning of the ownership transfer of a fairly significant chunk of Europe’s automotive manufacturing base to the Chinese,” says Simon Vessey, a car industry consultant.

Stellantis, owner of brands such as Vauxhall, Fiat and Peugeot, is leading the charge. It has the capacity to make at least 800,000 more cars in Europe than it can sell. So it is offering space to the Chinese.

Its factory in the French town of Rennes, will produce Voyah cars in a joint venture with state-owned carmaker Dongfeng, while two Spanish factories will start churning out vehicles with Leapmotor.

Other deals are in the offing. Nissan sold a Spanish factory to Chery, and is now in talks to share its plant in Sunderland. Ford also reportedly plans to sell part of a Spanish plant to Geely.

German media reports say Volkswagen (VW) has weighed up sharing its assembly lines with one or more Chinese companies, including SAIC and Xpeng. The company is said to have surplus capacity of 500,000 units at its European factories.

Oliver Blume, the chief executive of VW, said this week that the company had “currently no plans or discussions with Chinese manufacturers”.

However, Elvis Cheng, an executive at Xpeng, told a Financial Times conference earlier this month that his company had held talks with VW and other companies about “whether there is a suitable location here in Europe”.

BYD, the biggest of China’s behemoths, is building its own factory in Hungary. But it, too, has been knocking on the Europeans’ doors.

“We are talking to not only Stellantis, we’re talking to other companies too. We are looking for any available plant in Europe because we do want to utilise this kind of spare capacity,” Stella Li, the vice-president of BYD, said recently.

On the face of it, it’s a win-win. The Europeans have high fixed costs on these assembly lines, which they can bring down only by producing more cars.

“These plants are running far, far below where they should be. And there are really only two options. You can divert into manufacturing military kit. Or you can find somebody else who needs a car factory, and the Chinese are the only game in town,” says Vessey.

Handing the keys to the Chinese has a big advantage for the Europeans: it prevents expensive factory closures and politically unpalatable job losses.

The Europeans might also be able to cut costs by tapping into their Chinese partners’ cheaper supply chains.

Matthias Schmidt, the proprietor of Schmidt Automotive Research, says Stellantis’s Chinese deals should boost its bottom line, although “there’s a question about how much of the profit will filter back to China, and how much will remain in Amsterdam, at Stellantis”.

But Vessey says the Europeans’ short-run operational and financial gains are outweighed by the strategic wins to the Chinese.

He ticks off a list: “Regulatory access to the markets, because it’s local production. Ready-made industrial infrastructure, in markets where this is important. Ready-made labour relations frameworks, that they don’t have to think about. Supply chain access to the existing industrial system in Europe. And made-in-Europe brand legitimacy.”

‘Made in Europe’ drive

The deals could make sense for the European side, says Sander Tordoir, of the Centre for European Reform, but only if Brussels, Berlin and Paris ensure there are strings attached.

“It would be naive to sell Germany’s excess car plants to Chinese competitors without setting any type of conditions, both on value-add requirements to employ German workers and engineers, and for Chinese battery and EV producers to engage in technology transfer,” he says.

He predicts European leaders would be in a position to make these demands of the Chinese if they start signalling to Beijing now that they’re prepared to play hardball with tougher trade and industrial policies.

The EU has been making life more difficult for the Chinese.

The “Made in Europe” provisions in the bloc’s proposed Industrial Accelerator Act will prevent car makers from getting EU subsidies or tax breaks unless at least 70pc of an EV’s inputs, excluding the battery, are of European origin.

But ironically, says Vessey, it’s this very rule that has spurred the flurry of potential Chinese dealmaking regarding existing factories.

“The policy response was designed to protect the European manufacturing volume, but it is encouraging the Chinese to maybe come in with local manufacturing earlier than they would have otherwise done,” he says.

The push for Chinese tie-ups is still in its infancy. But if it takes hold, Schmidt envisages a future in which European car makers might do little more than pin their high-value badge to a cut-price Chinese chassis and engine.

“Stellantis’s Chinese joint ventures are a low-cost fundamental that sits underneath the skin, basically. Stellantis will then just hammer home branding, really push the brand image of Peugeot, Citroën, Fiat et al,” he says.

But Li said her company would even like to buy the brands. She described Stellantis’s Maserati marque as “very interesting”.

This sounds like kite-flying, at least for now. But it signals that the Chinese car makers’ march into Europe may still have plenty of mileage yet to come.

data.title: Motability under pressure to drop Chinese cars from benefits scheme
data.description:
A taxpayer-backed car scheme for people with disabilities is under pressure to drop Chinese brands over concerns it could harm British industry and boost Beijing.

MPs are preparing to write to Motability to call for the charity to drop cars made by manufacturers including BYD, Geely, Leapmotor and Chery.

The plans follow concerns that the Chinese car industry benefits from unfair subsidies from Beijing, which are allowing companies to unfairly undercut Western brands and hurting British industry.

Motability provides cars to disabled people in exchange for a portion of their benefits. A number of Chinese manufacturers have struck deals with the charity over the past two years as part of a wider push into the UK market.

The scheme, which also covers insurance, maintenance and breakdown cover, represents a lucrative sales channel. Motability accounts for around one fifth of all new car sales in the UK.

Campaigners and MPs claimed Motability was effectively offering a taxpayer subsidy to help these brands gain a foothold in Britain “at the expense of homegrown manufacturing”.

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, a former Tory leader, said: “Motability should make an effort to protect the British car industry. They should ban Chinese cars from the Motability scheme. Action needs to be taken or we won’t have a British car industry very soon.”

He added: “MPs are planning to write collectively to Motability to set out [demands] to protect the British car industry.”

Alicia Kearns, a Conservative MP and a former chairman of the foreign affairs select committee, said: “The Motability scheme should not become a backdoor subsidy for Chinese state-backed manufacturers looking to flood our market.

UK fourth biggest buyer of Chinese cars

“The Government must review whether this scheme, funded in part by the taxpayer, should be used to give them a foothold at the expense of homegrown manufacturing.”

Both the United States and European Union have imposed stiff tariffs on Chinese cars in response to claims of unfair support from Beijing.

However, the UK only imposes a standard 10pc duty, compared to levies of up to 45.3pc in the EU and tariffs of more than 100pc in the US.

The disparity has prompted a flood of Chinese brands to target the UK, with hundreds of dealerships opened over the last year. Britain is now the fourth biggest buyer of Chinese cars behind Russia, Mexico and the United Arab Emirates, according to a 2025 report from Global Intelligence Series (GIS).

Luke de Pulford, the executive director of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China (IPAC), said: “China has pursued a state strategy to try to dominate the world in electric vehicle manufacturing, and they’ve done so unfairly in violation of normal market practices.”

Referring to Motability’s deals with manufacturers, he said: “The stupidity of this is that not only are we allowing the Chinese state to effectively flood our markets with Chinese EVs, we’re using taxpayer money to pay for it.”

Luxury car controversy

Motability has been the subject of controversy before. Its inclusion of luxury cars including BMWs and Alfa Romeos provoked outcry and prompted an overhaul last year that saw it exclude many highly priced cars, including all coupés and convertibles.

A spokesman from Motability Operations, the company that runs the Motability Scheme, said: “Vehicles on the Scheme reflect what all UK drivers can get, excluding premium-brand cars, and we have a range of vehicles available to suit disabled people’s needs.

“As part of this choice, we recently announced an ambitious commitment to increase the number of British-built vehicles on the Scheme, which would also create demand for new UK manufacturing. This includes plans to double the number of Sunderland-built Nissan vehicles on the Scheme by 2030, and for half of vehicles leased to be British-built from 2035.”

Geely declined to comment. BYD, Chery and Leapmotor were contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trump backer’s Hollywood takeover threatens democracy, warn journalists
data.description:
The Warner Bros takeover by a billionaire Trump ally will pave the way for “improper political meddling”, leading US media figures have warned.

More than 200 academics, documentary makers and TV journalists have signed an open letter warning that the proposed takeover by David Ellison’s Paramount Skydance will have “severe consequences for American democracy”.

Warner Bros shareholders have backed the merger with Paramount, which will take control of the CNN news network as well as the historic Hollywood studios.

However, the letter, orchestrated by the Freedom of the Press Foundation, raised concerns about editorial intervention in CNN’s output, pointing to recent changes at CBS News, which was acquired by Mr Ellison as part of his $8bn (£6bn) takeover of Paramount last year.

Among the signatories is Jim Acosta, a former CNN host, and Laura Poitras, who won an Oscar for her documentary on Edward Snowden.

It accused CBS of making “sweeping changes” to appease Donald Trump, including pulling segments critical of the US president’s policies.

Mr Ellison held a recent dinner in Washington in honour of Mr Trump, while the US president has described the Paramount owner’s father, Larry Ellison, the Oracle billionaire, as a “good friend”.

‘Big fat bribe’

Several high-profile CBS reporters, including 60 Minutes host Anderson Cooper, have left the news network since Paramount installed Right-wing journalist Bari Weiss as its editor-in-chief.

The Late Show, hosted by Stephen Colbert, was cancelled after the presenter branded Paramount’s decision to settle a $16m lawsuit with Mr Trump shortly before the Ellison takeover was approved as a “big fat bribe”.

Paramount said the cancellation was “purely a financial decision” and “not related in any way to the show’s performance, content or other matters happening at Paramount”.

Sharyn Alfonsi, another veteran 60 Minutes correspondent, this week said she was pushed out of the network after clashing with executives over its output.

In an excoriating statement, Ms Alfonsi accused CBS of punishing her for “refusing to sanitise factually accurate reporting” and sending a “chilling message to the entire newsroom”.

The letter raised concerns about similar interference in CNN’s reporting.

It said: “With this track record, Ellison will likely alter CNN’s editorial direction (not to mention meddle with HBO’s documentaries) to be more friendly to the administration, threatening press freedom.

“We urge federal and state regulators and lawmakers, as well as Paramount shareholders and news consumers, to treat this merger not as an arms-length and above board business transaction, but as what it plainly is: a political arrangement to circumvent constitutional safeguards, with severe consequences for American democracy.”

It comes after Christiane Amanpour, CNN’s long-serving chief international anchor, this month raised concerns about the channel’s independence following the takeover.

Speaking at the Truth Tellers conference in London, she said: “Clearly I’m concerned ... I am obviously, as a person, as a journalist with a record, concerned.”

In addition to potential editorial interference, insiders fear sweeping job cuts if Paramount seeks to merge CNN with CBS. Mr Ellison’s company has said it will cut at least $6bn in costs after the deal.

Mr Ellison defended his takeover in a recent interview with CNBC, saying CNN was an “incredible brand with an incredible team, and we absolutely believe in the independence that needs to be maintained”.

He added: “We’re going to invest in the news business, and we think this transaction will be a positive for both CBS News and CNN.

“Editorial independence will absolutely be maintained. It’s maintained at CBS – it’ll be maintained at CNN.”

Thousands of Hollywood stars, including Emma Thompson, Mark Ruffalo and Ben Stiller, have also signed an open letter opposing the deal, warning that it could lead to fewer films being made and less choice for audiences.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The electric car takeover is now unstoppable
data.description:
Sign up here for more exclusive insight from Ambrose Evans-Pritchard in our Economic Intelligence newsletter, delivered direct to your inbox every Tuesday.

The world market for electric vehicles is moving into the parabolic phase of the technology S-curve, the critical stage where disruption moves with lightning speed and the mathematical compound effect demolishes the old order.

“Once you reach 5pc or 10pc, it takes off. The next stop is 30pc and suddenly you are on the way to 80pc,” said Kingsmill Bond, energy director at Ember and the man who coined the term “electrotech”.

New energy vehicles in China – EVs and plug-in hybrids – captured a record 61.4pc of total sales in April even though they are no longer exempt from purchase tax.

Sales of petrol and diesel cars crashed by 37pc from a year earlier, early evidence of the fallout from the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

We are all used to eye-watering figures from China by now. The more striking story is what is happening in the rest of the world, off the radar screen of US and European media.

Sales of EVs surpassed 63pc of the overall car market in Singapore last year, up from 3pc in 2020. The early evidence for April and May suggests the figure may already be near two thirds.

This is worth watching because the flourishing city-state is a bellwether of global positioning, a litmus test of whether the non-aligned world is pivoting towards Chinese electrotech or sticking with America’s fossil regime.

The core message of the International Energy Agency’s new report on the global EV industry is that the switch accelerated last year – despite the idiosyncratic counter-trend story in the US – and spread fastest in emerging markets.

Sales doubled across south-east Asia in 2025. The region is fast becoming a Chinese EV sphere – or in the case of Vietnam is developing its own copy-cat patriotic version with two fifths EV penetration spearheaded by VinFast, a Vietnamese automotive manufacturer.

South Korea jumped 65pc. Latin America has belatedly joined the rush with 75pc growth last year, led by Brazil and Mexico.

This shift to EVs no longer has anything to do with climate policy, though it is driven in part by the quest for cleaner air in the unbreathable smog-cursed cities of the Global South.

Last year’s surge was driven chiefly by market forces. The IEA said 70pc of battery EVs sold in China in 2025 were already cheaper than petrol and diesel cars on purchase cost alone, even before lifecycle savings on fuel.

It is a near total wipe-out in the Chinese market for small cars. The newer, cheaper EVs are now reaching the world market, either as direct exports or manufactured at Chinese-owned plants at regional hubs in Thailand, Brazil or Hungary.

Active intervention is required to slow the process, as the US is now doing in one of the most extraordinary efforts to turn back the technology clock in global economic history.

MAGA America has made a political choice to block EV penetration at the 10pc threshold, both by imposing a prohibitive tariff on imports and by finding all kinds of ways to throw sand into the gears.

It is a strangely defeatist retreat since America once led the world with Tesla and is still the leader in the future technology of solid-state batteries.

It could ultimately beat China at its own EV game with a manufacturing Manhattan Project – which Joe Biden was trying to do (clumsily) with the now gutted Inflation Reduction Act – but to capitalise on that, it needs an EV industry at scale and a flourishing ecosystem of EV suppliers.

It is not widely understood but world demand for oil in transport peaked in 2019 and is already in absolute decline. The war in the Gulf is now pushing the process. It is hard to conclude that Donald Trump’s “excursion” is anything other than an utter disaster for the long-term interests of Opec, Russia and shale frackers in Texas.

“This is the first time we’ve ever had an oil crisis when there is an alternative on hand and it is easily available,” said Bond.

“Battery costs are plummeting and everything is coming together. The whole of Asia is now electrifying and that is the soft underbelly of the global fossil system.”

There is no return to the status quo ante. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards have acquired de facto taxing powers and political control over ships leaving the Gulf, even if this is dressed up as an “environmental protection fee” – a gem of Iranian humour.

Such an arrangement is poison for security of supply and the credibility of the oil and gas industry. Seaborne shipments of energy – or indeed any commodity – are no longer safe in the “new joule order” where the US navy can no longer be relied on to uphold free navigation and has instead joined the pirates.

Four fifths of the world’s population live in oil-importing countries. For them it is now an urgent economic and national security imperative to break the dependency once and for all.

EV sales in Europe have been held back by the foot-dragging of the big car companies and their focus on bourgeois models with a fatter profit margin. A whole range of workaday cars in the €17,000 (£14,700) to €25,000 range are finally hitting the market: the Dacia Spring, Citroën ë-C3, Renault Twingo E-Tech and the VW ID.2.

The chaos of the charging system is diminishing. All new chargers installed in the EU since 2024 must accept regular debit cards. Drivers will soon no longer have to contend with a plethora of separate apps and company payment cards.

The latest data from E-Mobility Europe show that EV sales jumped by 34pc in April from a year earlier.

The take-off effect that began in Norway – with state help – is spreading to Denmark (82pc), Finland (49pc), Sweden (41pc), Netherlands (37pc), Belgium (36pc), Germany and France (26pc).

Italy has been a laggard but is finally starting to catch up with a 97pc jump in EV registrations this year, despite electricity prices even higher than in Britain.

Widely repeated claims that consumers don’t want to buy EVs will one day be studied by social historians as a form of collective false consciousness, a distortion created by the culture war and fed by large doses of black propaganda by Russian bots and Western vested interests.

A temporary slowdown in the rate of global EV growth morphed into media headlines of a sales “collapse”. Too much attention was paid to America and not enough attention to Asia or the world. Little of it was ever really true.

We can now conclude with near absolute certainty that the electrification of transport is unstoppable and will happen even faster than earlier expected. The S-curve is alive and well.

data.title: Burnham in row over plan to build on green belt
data.description:
Andy Burnham has been accused of trying to trick residents who oppose his controversial plans to build housing on Greater Manchester’s green belt.

The Mayor of Greater Manchester is trying to build around 12,500 homes on Bury’s green belt. But residents are largely opposed to the plans and have been fighting them for around a decade.

The Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA), headed by Mr Burnham, then proposed to offset the lost green land through a complicated field swap agreement.

But campaigners claimed the 49 areas that would be added to the green belt are “unusable” and of “low quality”. Documents seen by The Telegraph show that the land comprises playing fields, owned golf courses and dense woodland.

Stephen Cluer, who runs the Bury Folk Keep It Green campaign fighting the development, accused Mr Burnham of trying to “trick local residents” into believing that he was protecting the Bury green belt.

He said: “Andy Burnham came up with the idea that he could reduce the net loss of the green belt by reallocating and protecting other non-green belt land to compensate for the losses. But the proposed land was unusable and of low quality like steep valleys and landfill sites.

“It was a complete sleight of hand tactic to try and trick local residents into believing that Andy Burnham was protecting the Bury green belt. But it was all smoke and mirrors.”

‘Deeply concerning’

Matt Vickers, the Tory deputy chairman, said: “Andy Burnham’s disdain for the green belt should be deeply concerning for the millions who love our green spaces up and down the country, as I do.

“Using cheap tactics to reclassify land so he can fudge his numbers shows Burnham really does not care about protecting these green spaces.

“Andy Burnham is more of the same. The rest of the Labour Party do not want to protect the green belt either, so it is little surprise that the front-runner to be Starmer’s successor believes the same.”

In 2019, almost 200 residents staged a protest outside Bury Town Hall where they confronted Mr Burnham and shouted “traitor” and “shame on you”.

At the time, he told protesters that the housing numbers were as low as they could be, saying: “My aim has been to reduce it as much as I possibly can but if you look at what the Government are saying, they are saying I’ve gone too low. So, if I could reduce it further, I would. But I’m under pressure.”

The row comes as Mr Burnham attempts to return to Westminster by running as the Labour candidate in the Makerfield by-election next month.

If he wins, he is expected to mount a leadership challenge against Sir Keir Starmer, who is fighting to save his premiership amid a party revolt.

A GMCA spokesman said: “Places for Everyone is key to addressing the needs of our growing city region while protecting as much green belt as possible and maximising the use of brownfield land.

“Having this joint plan is the best line of defence against unplanned development and is essential to delivering the new homes, jobs, and infrastructure our communities need.

“Our approach has been transparent and subject to independent checks. Over the past decade, we’ve received more than 40,000 responses to our plans. Last year, a High Court judge dismissed in full a legal challenge by green belt campaigners.”

data.title: Heavy rain risks even more delays to HS2
data.description:
Heavy rain risks delaying the opening of HS2, the project’s chief executive has warned.

Mark Wild, the head of HS2 Limited, said downpours could scupper the construction of bridges and earthworks that must be completed before any track can be laid.

This risks delaying the project’s launch by at least a year, he told the transport committee.

HS2 Limited is targeting an opening date in 2036, although Mr Wild said this could slip to as late as 2039, depending on factors including the weather.

The troubled high-speed railway is already a decade late and £50bn over budget.

Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, said on Tuesday that the project could now cost as much as £102.7bn, equating to £1bn per mile of railway line.

Mr Wild was questioned by MPs during a committee hearing on Wednesday, during which he warned about the impact of wet weather during key earthwork-building periods between March and November.

Mr Wild said HS2 Limited must finish the 30pc of outstanding work and begin laying track by 2029 if trains were to start running by a “stretching” target date of 2036 set by Labour this week.

He said: “The next four years are pivotal in this programme. One of the big drivers of the three-year range is do we need three, four, five years to complete the civil engineering?

“The question is, can we get the civil engineering done in three earthworks seasons? If we did have a really difficult summer of deluge of rain, we might need another year.”

Asked by Scott Arthur, an MP, whether HS2 Limited was “hiding behind” the weather to buy itself time, Mr Wild, who took over as chief in December 2024, said he did not want to cut corners.

The chief executive said that while the doubling of costs for HS2 in five years was “terrible news”, he was confident it would open by 2039 and within the revised budget.

He said: “I know I’m promising jam tomorrow, but I’m convinced the bookends we’ve created here are robust. It will need HS2 to have the right capability and the supply chain to lean into it. Both of those things I believe will happen.”

Once heavy works have been completed, laying down the tracks should be like a “factory environment” and a “relatively straightforward and sequential job to get the railway open”.

Mr Wild said HS2 Limited had “lost control of the programme” by the time he took over, with managers not even sure what work had been completed.

Asked whether it would ever have been possible to build HS2 within a decade and at a cost of £43bn, as originally envisaged, he pointed to France’s completion of the 190-mile TGV line to Bordeaux in six years for €7.8bn (£6.8bn).

He said: “If you go to France and Spain, they do projects of this scale in faster time. They have done it because they have got the consent, the design, the supply chain all lined up.”

Mr Wild said that reducing HS2’s top speed to just under 200mph from 223mph would not only save £2.5bn by simplifying the testing and commissioning of trains, but remove the risk that delivering such speeds might not be possible.

He said the route would still have to be built to the highest specification, however, since it had progressed so far that any changes would cost more money.

Lord Hendy, the rail minister, told the committee that while he was confident in the ability of HS2 Limited’s new management, “anybody listening to this would be rightly horrified”.

He said: “Taxpayers ought to be furious, as we are, that we are in this position. This is a disastrous place to be with a project at this stage.”

Lord Hendy said the “most astonishing” folly was a plan to operate trains on HS2 using an automatic signalling system that excludes the driver and which “has not been invented”.

data.title: Brace for higher smartphone prices, warns BT
data.description:
BT has warned of higher smartphone prices as the  AI boom drives up the cost of memory chips.

Allison Kirkby, BT’s chief executive, said the former state monopoly was bracing for potential semiconductor shortages as soon as this year as tech giants were buying up the technology to power AI data centres.

The squeeze on supplies threatens to push up the price of equipment – such as Apple’s iPhone – sold by network operators including BT.

Ms Kirkby said: “I’m sure Apple will do their best to minimise any supply chain weaknesses but it’s common knowledge that the chip market is under demand by all the rise in AI.

“We are working very proactively with our suppliers – it’s mainly handsets, it could be in a bit of routers as well – and I’m sure the industry with its partners will do the best it can to minimise the impact on pricing in the marketplace.”

She added: “But with chip shortages everywhere, that will put pressure on pricing in certain parts of the market, not just in our sector going forward.”

Ms Kirkby said that while she had not yet seen any price increases, she expected manufacturers such as Apple to pass on higher costs to consumers across the industry.

BT said the potential chip shortages and the effect on prices will contribute to a drop in turnover in the coming year as shoppers choose not to upgrade or steer clear of buying new phones altogether.

The company expects revenues of between £19bn and £19.5bn in the year ahead, down from £19.7bn last year. The warning was issued as the FTSE 100 group also unveiled plans to ramp up its cost-cutting as part of a broader turnaround plan.

BT has boosted its target for cost cuts from £3bn to £3.7bn, while the savings programme has also been extended by a year to 2030.

Ms Kirkby, who took up the role in 2024, charmed investors with a promise to cut costs and increase returns to investors as the telecoms giant nears the end of its investment in full-fibre broadband.

BT stripped out £580m in costs last year, taking its total savings in the last two years to £1.5bn. The company is laying off tens of thousands of employees, largely because of a reduction in engineers but also as roles are replaced with AI.

Ms Kirkby said the increased cost-saving target reflects that the headcount reduction will be at the higher end of its previously announced plan to cut up to 55,000 jobs.

However, the telecoms chief is now under pressure to return BT to growth in an increasingly competitive market.

The company is planning to spend tens of millions of pounds attempting to revive BT Mobile after an abandoned effort to make EE the company’s flagship brand under Ms Kirkby’s predecessor.

Revenue fell 3pc to £19.7bn last year because of lower handset sales and as the company made less money from its customers. However, pre-tax profit rose 8pc to £1.4bn as cost-cutting paid off.

BT shed 825,000 customers from its Openreach broadband network last year amid fierce competition from start-up rivals known as alt-nets, though this was better than the 850,000 losses forecast. Bosses expect to lose a further 800,000 customers this year.

BT, which enjoyed its fastest-ever quarter of network build at the end of last year, reaches 23 million UK premises with its full-fibre broadband network and is on track to reach its target of 25 million by the end of this year.

Bosses reiterated a target of £2bn in free cash flow by the end of the year and £3bn by the end of the decade. The company also increased its full-year dividend to 8.32p per share.

Ms Kirkby said: “We’re pleased with the progress we’ve made but we’re also clear there’s much more to do to realise BT’s full potential.”

Separately on Thursday, Samsung, the Korean electronics giant, said staff in its semiconductor division would share a 40tn won (£20bn) payout after striking a last-minute deal with unions to avoid a strike.

The roughly 78,000 workers are expected to get an average of K₩513m each, but individual payouts are likely to vary, according to Bloomberg.

data.title: Lloyds Bank bans trans women from using female lavatories
data.description:
Britain’s biggest bank has told trans women not to use female lavatories after the equalities watchdog said single-sex spaces should be defined by biology.

Lloyds Banking Group, which has 28 million customers across the UK, has told staff to use bathrooms that correspond with their biological sex rather than their chosen gender.

The banking giant updated its policies after Britain’s equalities watchdog issued new guidance last week. The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) said single sex spaces must be used on the basis of biological sex.

The new guidance applies to all organisations that provide public services, with a future update planned for private sector employers.

Not all lenders have adjusted their policies. A spokesman for Coventry Building Society, which also owns the Co-op Bank, said: “We are in the process of reviewing our guidance.”

‘An unsettling day’

The EHRC update was issued in the wake of the Supreme Court’s landmark ruling last April, in which it determined that the words “man” and “woman” refer to a person’s biological sex, instead of their gender, under the Equality Act.

In the immediate aftermath of the Supreme Court’s judgment, Lloyds Bank executives pledged “solidarity” with trans staff.

In a post on Lloyds Bank’s intranet last year, Andrew Walton, the bank’s chief of corporate affairs, wrote: “Thought I would come on here today with a note of support for our trans and non-binary colleagues on what I know will be an unsettling day following the UK Supreme Court decision.”

Lloyds Banking Group, which owns Lloyds Bank, Halifax, Bank of Scotland and Scottish Widows, has 65,000 staff.

The lender initially held off on changing its bathroom policies even as rivals adjusted theirs. Barclays said it would prohibit trans women from using female bathrooms shortly after the Supreme Court judgment.

Speaking to reporters at the time, CS Venkatakrishnan, Barclays’ chief executive, said: “Following the Supreme Court ruling ... we believe that we have to comply with that by not allowing trans women to use female bathrooms.”

A series of employment tribunal cases have since highlighted the perils faced by organisations that continue to let people use bathrooms based on their chosen gender.

A Muslim woman employed by NHS England won a discrimination case earlier this month after a trans colleague was allowed to use female toilets.

It is understood Lloyds Bank will also provide “gender-inclusive” unisex bathrooms.

A report from campaign group Sex Matters, published earlier this month, found leading banks including HSBC and NatWest were continuing to let staff use bathrooms based on their preferred gender.

Lloyds Banking Group declined to comment. HSBC and NatWest were approached for comment.

data.title: The adviser at the centre of BP’s boardroom battle
data.description:
Speaking at an event in London last month, long-serving BP executive Ben Mathews was keen to stress that he was not one to sit silently on the sidelines.

When it comes to team meetings, “there shouldn’t be any mice in the room”, he said. “We have a role to play. In the roles I’ve had, there has been an expectation that I contribute to the debate.”

Just weeks later, Mathews’ contribution at BP, where he serves as company secretary, has been thrust into the spotlight.

The 59-year-old has been described as a key driving force behind a boardroom battle at the oil major, having clashed with Albert Manifold in the run-up to the BP chairman’s dramatic defenestration this week amid claims of bullying.

Manifold has strongly refuted the allegations. He has claimed they are “lies” and accused individuals of hiding behind “anonymity”.

But the row has now cast a spotlight on the little-known Mathews and the growing power wielded by the once arcane role of company secretary. As Mathews himself put it just a month ago: “As a company secretary, you see it all.”

Mathews, who began his career at PwC, where he qualified as a chartered company secretary, has stayed loyal to the profession throughout his career. Beginning at BG Group, the oil and gas giant that was bought by Shell for $70bn (£52bn) in 2016, he subsequently moved to mining firm Rio Tinto and then HSBC before joining BP in 2019.

As company secretary, Mathews occupies a unique role in BP’s hierarchy and is responsible for providing independent advice to the board on issues such as corporate governance, legal duties and regulatory requirements. He sits on the board and reports directly to the chairman, acting as a conduit between the board and the executive team.

One of his critical responsibilities in recent months was to help with preparations for BP’s annual general meeting in April – the first where Manifold held the role of chairman following his appointment in September.

The meeting was a disaster for the chairman, with almost a fifth of shareholders voting against his re-election. Legal & General, one of Europe’s biggest asset managers, said before the AGM that it would oppose Manifold, while Glass Lewis, a proxy adviser, recommended investors do the same.

In an embarrassing defeat, shareholders also rejected BP’s attempts to move to online AGMs and scrap the publication of company-specific climate reports.

However, it is Mathews’ apparent role in Manifold’s acrimonious departure this week that has propelled the company secretary into the public eye.

The two men clashed in the run-up to Manifold’s exit, with Mathews raising concerns over the Irish businessman’s behaviour.

The board of BP this week said it was ousting Manifold after discovering “issues it deems unacceptable” regarding his alleged conduct and corporate governance. A source close to the board said Manifold’s dismissal followed allegations of “bullying” and “verbal abuse” of staff. He was also said to be “shouty” and have a “volcanic” temper.

The source said much of this behaviour had been directed at Mathews, often in front of other staff.

In a lengthy statement published on Thursday, Manifold, who was previously the chief executive of the building materials company CRH, said he had “never once had accusations made against me such as those made in recent days”. He is said to have been left “shell-shocked” by the allegations.

Perhaps more telling, however, were suggestions that profligate spending was at the heart of his dispute with BP and, more specifically, its top team.

“Where I saw unnecessary or excessive expenditure, I called it out,” he wrote. “I had no interest in having a dedicated chauffeur-driven limousine at my beck and call on the occasions that I was in London.”

Manifold, who is considering legal action against BP, added: “I, like most people, walked, took taxis, trains, etc. I had no interest in taking private aviation nor in availing myself of corporate tickets for sports events. I made my own coffee and bought my lunch in the local café. I sat in a small office, eschewing the grand corner-office privilege of previous chairmen.”

The ousted chairman praised new chief executive Meg O’Neill, who he described as “ambitious and very talented”, and finance chief Kate Thomson. Pointedly, however, there was no mention of BP’s board.

As the official in charge of governance issues, Mathews is thought to have been at the heart of this clash over costs.

A source close to Manifold said: “It’s not wrong to view Mathews as a driver of this, and there was certainly an argument with him over the approach to costs.”

Meanwhile, a source familiar with the BP board played down claims of an argument about costs and said the clash was primarily related to the chairman’s “personal conduct”.

The source added: “Ben has great integrity and he’s used to tough corporate environments. His former boss at HSBC was Sir Mark Tucker, who is known as a really hard driving guy.

“He is not the only person who had complaints, they were from more than one person, senior people and some who are more junior.”

It is not the first incident of boardroom turmoil under Mathews’ watch. Previous chairman Helge Lund stepped down last year amid a shareholder revolt over BP’s green energy pledge. That was compounded by the departure of chief executive Murray Auchincloss after less than two years and his predecessor Bernard Looney, who resigned over revelations about workplace relationships.

There is no evidence that Mathews, who has been granted medical leave due to stress, played a role in those departures as well.

Nevertheless, the latest saga raises questions over whether BP risks sacrificing shareholder value on the altar of corporate governance.

Manifold, who built CRH from its modest Dublin roots into a global building materials giant, has acknowledged he may have been forthright in some of his dealings at BP.

“Is it possible that in my determination to drive change on costs, performance, the balance sheet and shareholder communications, I pushed hard and challenged people directly? Yes, it is,” he said. “But there is a considerable distance between driving an organisation with urgency and the characterisation of my conduct that is now being put about.”

In Manifold’s telling, his no-nonsense approach was simply too much for BP’s governance-obsessed board to handle.

Other high-profile members of BP’s board include Dame Amanda Blanc, chief executive of insurance giant Aviva, and Johannes Teyssen, the former boss of German energy group E.On and Lufthansa chairman.

Hina Nagarajan, the president of Diageo Africa, and Satish Pai, who leads one of the world’s largest aluminium rolling and recycling companies, Hindalco Industries, also sit on the board.

BP has said it stands by its statement on Manifold’s behaviour, adding: “We have a duty of care to all our employees, particularly those impacted by his behaviour.”

Still, Manifold’s abrupt departure has left BP with yet another leadership vacuum at a time when the company is grappling for direction and raises serious questions about the board’s oversight.

“It’s very messy and it’s not a good look for the board,” says Ashley Kelty, an analyst at Panmure Liberum. “It essentially shows apart from Meg O’Neill and the CFO, you’ve got a board that arguably isn’t fit for purpose.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: JCB billionaire: We won’t let China smother us
data.description:
The billionaire owner of JCB has vowed to fight off Chinese efforts to “smother” British industry.

Lord Bamford said he will not sit back while Beijing stages a takeover of the global digger market like it has done in car production with its flood of cheap electric vehicles (EVs).

He said JCB still had a technological edge over Chinese rivals, citing a £100m expansion into hydrogen power, which he said would one day replace diesel as a source of fuel for some of his machinery.

However, the businessman criticised successive UK governments for failing to match the aggressive support that Beijing provides to its own manufacturing industry.

He said: “One has to respect enormously the involvement of the Chinese government.

“They get involved with manufacturers in their country to try and end up with a dominant position as quick as they can. We certainly, as a country, don’t do anything like they do.

“They’ve been very good at copying in the first instance and have copied our machines and other people’s machines.

“But it is the combination of all the incentives and all the involvement that has made a difference, and it is not really understood by normal British politicians or civil servants.”

Lord Bamford said the speed at which China has come to dominate the EV car market should serve as a warning that other sectors would also follow suit unless countries are up to the fight.

More than one in four EVs sold in Britain last year was made in China, while BYD, a leading brand, overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest EV producer.

He said: “This is what they have done to a whole industry. The car industry in Europe is almost decimated in how long? Only three years, something like that.”

Western firms are capable of holding the line, he said, but need more political backing if they are to do so.

He said: “It depends whether we just lie there and let them smother us. I certainly won’t be doing that with our products.

“We sell on a worldwide basis; we compete against companies from Japan and from China, and we feel we have product advantages, of which hydrogen can be one.”

Chinese construction equipment manufacturers, including XCMG, Sany, Zoomlion and LiuGong, are seeking to undercut JCB on price while rapidly expanding their global dealer networks.

Barriers to business

However, instead of support, Lord Bamford – a donor to both the Conservatives and Reform UK – said Labour was threatening family firms such as JCB, which employs 20,000 people, with punishing inheritance taxes.

His eldest son, Jo, told City AM last month that JCB “could quite easily become an American business” if backed into a corner by the Government.

Lord Bamford said he had no wish to withdraw JCB from Britain – which is still based within a few miles of the lock-up garage in Staffordshire where it was founded 80 years ago – but confirmed that the worries were genuine.

“I think it’s a real concern to virtually all the family businesses,” he said. “Britain is our base, and I’m British too, and we certainly want to stay that way.

“But you have to look at what happens in other countries as well. It’s more an attitude towards doing business.”

There is a range of barriers facing millions of Britain’s smaller businesses, he said, including electricity costs “four times those in America” and challenges in securing planning permission.

“Nobody bothers about those businesses,” he said. “We have 800 suppliers, and we would like more.

“But most of them have disappeared; they have gone out of manufacturing, sadly. They are not encouraged, they are actively discouraged in my view.

“They need to have the oxygen to grow and compete, not just in their town but in the country and to go outside of the country too.”

He added: “It’s not just the Labour Party, it is the Conservatives just as much. There should be lots of people like us, competing around the world. We used to do so as a country.”

JCB is already ratcheting up production in the US in order to minimise the impact of Donald Trump’s steel tariffs, with a new $500m (£375m), 400-acre plant employing 1,500 people expected to open in Texas in October.

Lord Bamford said: “I think Mr Trump is an incredible man, and he’s having an incredible impact, good or bad.

“As a way of making money for his country, he’s making trillions of dollars every day with imports. And we export a lot, and we are paying a lot of money every day to America.”

The tycoon, who turned 80 last year, said the surge in oil prices triggered by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz was unlikely to impact demand for construction equipment, with the cost of diesel not usually the key factor in purchase decisions.

Hydrogen bet

The Iran war could even have a positive impact for JCB in spurring sales of hydrogen-fuelled diggers, the first of which was launched last week.

He said: “Just think of a country like India. They have to import all their gas and all their oil. And there’s a war going on with Iran, and they’re not getting any. Other alternatives have to be looked at, of which hydrogen is a very serious one.”

Lord Bamford said JCB first began looking into alternatives to diesel seven or eight years ago as the drive to cut carbon emissions began to dominate the political agenda.

After considering the potential for battery-powered machines – of which JCB does make some smaller models – it became apparent that hydrogen burned in a traditional engine as a substitute for diesel was a better bet, he said.

He said: “When hydrogen is used in an internal combustion engine, you end up with zero emissions. And for most applications, it is as good, if not better. It is a pretty efficient way of producing power.

“There have been all sorts of problems along the way; we’re developing a new technology. But so far no major impediments.”

JCB announced an attempt to break the world land-speed record for hydrogen propulsion last week in a mission to publicise the potential of the technology.

Andy Green, an ex-RAF fighter pilot, will attempt to set a new best of more than 350mph later this year on the Bonneville Salt Flats in Utah in a 32ft-long car called the Hydromax and powered by two JCB hydrogen engines producing 1,600 horsepower.

The retired wing commander set the diesel record 20 years ago using JCB engines and is named the fastest man on land in the Guinness Book of Records for his 763mph run in the jet-engine-powered Thrust SuperSonic in 1997.

Lord Bamford said he found the challenges of advanced engineering and projects such as the record attempt as captivating as ever.

“It helps me get out of bed in the morning,” said the billionaire, who revealed over the weekend that his second son George Bamford would eventually succeed him at the helm of the family business.

data.title: Surprise, surprise: 20mph zones are a money-making scam
data.description:
Have you been driving 32 per cent more dangerously than you were in 2022? Well, that’s what the latest figures suggest. Nearly one million drivers received points on their licence last year, up 32 per cent from the 678,367 who did in 2022. Drivers were also reportedly hit with a record 5.6 million penalty points on their licences in 2025.

I’m one of the new “bad” drivers. After years without points, I got three this February and three in March, for speeding on a motorway and on a B road. In both cases, I was in Wales – the new natural home of the speed camera and the 20mph speed limit.

In both cases, too, I was driving perfectly safely on near-empty roads – even if I was admittedly a little over the speed limit. But that isn’t the point of the new road regime. Safe driving is never taken into account. Speeding is a strict liability offence.

Of course people haven’t got any worse at driving over the last few years. But there has been a blanket roll-out of 20mph zones across much of Britain, including Wales. Suddenly, on Welsh roads where I’ve been driving safely for 37 years, a maddening limit has been introduced. What a money-making scam it’s turned out to be.

These are wide, empty roads in Pembrokeshire, one of the most remote parts of the United Kingdom – roads where you don’t see another car for hours at a time. On the steep, deserted hills leading down to the coast, I quite often go over 20mph on my bicycle – thank God the speed cameras don’t pick me up.

More than a sixth of British roads now have a 20mph speed limit – 39,000 miles of the country’s 246,500-mile road network. The limit was imposed arbitrarily, with no concession to the local landscape, size of population or driving conditions. Most of London is now covered by the restriction too, no matter how wide, empty and free of nearby pedestrians a road might be.

Of course, speed offences don’t just bring points. They are a mammoth cash cow producing hundreds of millions of pounds in fines a year. The Government and local councils have a double defence against charges of milking motorists. They argue that, on environmental and safety grounds, draconian 20 mph speed limits can only be a good thing.

Eco-warriors bash the car and praise the train. But have you tried to rely on a train for regular commuting in Britain in recent years? Unreliable, expensive and, increasingly, populated by morons blaring out their music and conversation at top volume.

And, in any case, since Dr Beeching’s cuts to the railways 60 years ago, less and less of Britain is served by railways. In much of Britain, buses are non-existent. Will no one in government – local or national – speak in favour of the speedy car (one of the greatest instruments of freedom of the last 140 years)?

Particularly in the country, only the car can get you from A to B on time, in comfort, at speed, in the company of quiet non-morons. And only petrol or hybrid will do if the distance is more than 200 miles, because such a journey is usually too far for a single electric charge.

Sophisticated, well-off urbanites can survive without a car. In a city like London, you’re spoilt for choice, with tubes, buses, trains, electric bikes and a proliferation of taxi companies. Much of rural Britain has none of those services. The car is the only answer.

Yet the Government goes on bashing the motorist with fines, speed cameras and massively overtaxed fuel. It’s even seemingly doing its best to abolish the stuff that powers petrol engines.

Who would now dare publish the Shell Guides? Initially edited by the literary titan John Betjeman, they were the best guides to British architecture – but also sponsored by a behemoth oil company.

For the 50 years of their publication, driving (at well over 20mph) was celebrated by poets, artists, drivers and the Government as a way to unlock Britain and give you carte blanche to roam the country for work and pleasure.

The car is still king. There is still no better way to get about, given the travails of public transport. And yet the Government is determined to fine us, punish us, slow us down and milk us dry as we stick to the only true form of independent, long-distance travel.

Harry Mount is author of How England Made the English (Penguin)

data.title: France calls for Britain to receive EU car subsidies
data.description:
France has opened the door to British-made cars receiving subsidies from the EU in a dramatic reversal of its previous opposition.

Inclusion in the bloc’s “Made in Europe” programme would enable UK electric cars and components to qualify for subsidies and tax breaks, levelling the playing field with European rivals.

The French government had been a vocal advocate of restricting UK carmakers’ access to the scheme to the EU’s 27 member states. But Nicolas Forissier, the trade minister, suggested it could now support Britain’s inclusion.

He told the Financial Times: “We will have to clarify this problem. They don’t belong to the EU, but they are very close neighbours.

“Of course, they’re very much integrated, so how do we solve this? It should be discussed. I think it’s an important issue.”

The French concession comes after a Europe-wide lobbying effort by Sir Chris Bryant, the UK trade minister.

Sir Chris told The Telegraph: “In my conversations with my French counterparts, with [industry minister Sébastien] Martin and Mr Forissier, there was quite a keen acceptance that Made in Europe would also include Made in the UK.”

The EU is looking for ways to protect its domestic industries as it braces for an economic hit from Donald Trump’s war with Iran.

Luis de Guindos, the European Central Bank vice-president, on Wednesday warned that the war risks triggering a financial crisis.

He added that the world economy and financial markets had been remarkably resilient so far, but could be tested if the war drags on.

Mr de Guindos said: “While the full impact of the war is unclear at this stage, the repercussions for the global economy and financial stability are becoming graver the longer it lasts.”

The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz – a key waterway through which a fifth of the world’s oil and gas passed before Mr Trump’s attack on Iran – has already driven up energy prices and hamstrung economic growth.

But the central bank boss warned this could spill over into a broader financial crunch. He said: “Despite initial declines, financial asset prices still look stretched by historical standards, all the more so when current geo-economic stress and uncertainty are taken into account.

“This leaves markets vulnerable to sharp repricing, and non-bank financial institutions could amplify financial market swings if they were to occur.”

Britain is already eligible for inclusion in the EU’s Made in Europe scheme thanks to its free trade agreement with Brussels. But under current proposals, corporate fleet vehicles – which comprise 60pc of the European auto market – and small electric cars are excluded.

To be eligible for subsidies, tax breaks and public procurement tenders, these cars would have to be assembled within the EU with up to 70pc of components sourced from the bloc.

The Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders last month sounded the alarm because of the rules, warning that they would put Britain’s car industry at a “major disadvantage” and that production would decline as a result.

Nissan has reportedly warned it would shut its Sunderland plant if Britain is not fully included in the scheme, while Jaguar Land Rover and Toyota could also be affected. The UK-EU automotive trade is worth €80bn (£69bn) a year.

Threat of being ‘economically dangerous’

EU ministers meet on Thursday to discuss the so-called Industrial Accelerator Act, which sets out the scheme. Sir Chris said his lobbying effort had included Poland, Germany, the Czech Republic, Austria and Spain.

He told ministers that excluding Britain would also harm European carmakers because of the close integration of supply chains.

He said: “There are very complex supply chains across the whole of the European Union. The classic instance is that most French cars made by Renault have British braking systems in them.

“Do we really want to disrupt all of that, or is that going to be as economically dangerous for France and Italy and Spain and so on as it will be for the UK?”

Sir Chris added that excluding the UK would make European carmakers less competitive against “other parts of the world”.

Chinese carmakers are seeking to skirt the Made in Europe restrictions by scooping up European carmakers’ factories on the Continent.

Mr Forissier has also publicly fretted about the threat from China, telling Euronews last week that “we must no longer be naive”. He said he wanted to see the EU require its manufacturers to diversify their inputs away from any dependency on China.
