data.title: London house prices slump to lowest level in five years
data.description:
London house prices have slumped to their lowest level since 2021 as higher taxes bite.

Prices fell by 2.1pc in the capital in the 12 months to March, according to Office for National Statistics (ONS) data.

This marked the eighth month in a row in which year-on-year prices decreased in London, with prices having fallen by £2,000 since February.

The average sale price of £542,000 was the lowest recorded since October 2021, and has dropped by £12,000 since March 2025.

The figures revealed a continued softening of London’s property market, which has been Britain’s “weakest performer” for some time, according to Aneisha Beveridge, of estate agents Hamptons.

She said: “In inner London, prices have fallen in 28 of the last 36 months and are now broadly back to 2019 levels, as higher stamp duty costs and wider tax changes have weighed most heavily on higher-value markets.

“However, softer conditions are now spreading more widely across the capital. Higher borrowing costs and the expense of moving are continuing to limit activity, meaning those who don’t need to move are increasingly sitting tight.”

London has historically led Britain’s housing market, but house prices have struggled over the last year amid Labour’s tax raids.

The capital’s market has been particularly hit by the Government’s changes to non-dom status and higher stamp duty rates.

In November, Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, also announced that from 2028 a council tax surcharge would be applied to houses worth £2m or more.

London is expected to be hit the hardest by the tax raid. Of the top 20 constituencies with the highest share of £2m-plus homes, 16 were in London and four were in the South East.

Hamptons has warned that properties worth £2m could see a 5pc price correction as a result of the so-called “mansion tax”.

ONS data showed that there was also a smaller decline in property prices of 0.6pc across England, where the average price was £290,000, compared with a year earlier. Average UK house prices remained unchanged.

Jeremy Leaf, a North London estate agent and former Royal Institute of Chartered Surveyors residential chairman, said: “The data shows that the Iran war is catching up with buyers and sellers with its inevitable impact on confidence.

“Overall, on the ground, sales are proceeding but more slowly and taking much longer to complete, with price reductions and renegotiations commonplace.”

The war in Iran has pushed up mortgage rates as lenders predict that soaring energy prices will revive inflation and force central banks to raise interest rates.

On Tuesday, the average mortgage rate was 5.64pc up from 4.91pc three months before, according to Moneyfacts.

The ONS said that the annual UK house price inflation rate had slowed in part because average monthly prices fell by 0.4pc between February and March this year, compared with a large monthly rise of 1.2pc in the same period last year.

This rise in 2025 happened before changes to stamp duty in England and Northern Ireland, which lowered the thresholds at which the tax was due, with buyers rushing to complete before April.

data.title: JP Morgan boss: AI means we’ll need fewer bankers
data.description:
The boss of JP Morgan has said the Wall Street giant will need fewer bankers in the future as a result of AI.

Jamie Dimon said the technology would “reduce some of our jobs down the road” in comments likely to fuel anxieties about a downturn in white-collar jobs.

“AI is going to change jobs. It will reduce some of our jobs down the road,” Mr Dimon said in a Bloomberg Television interview at the bank’s China summit in Shanghai on Wednesday.

“I think we will be hiring more AI people and probably fewer bankers in certain categories.”

JP Morgan, the largest bank in the US, employs more than 300,000 people in 100 countries, including more than 20,000 in Britain.

Mr Dimon made the comments just days after Bill Winters, Standard Chartered’s boss, attracted backlash after saying AI will wipe out “lower-value human capital”.

Standard Chartered said it would cut nearly 8,000 jobs, or around 15pc of its workforce, as AI agents increasingly replace back-office staff.

After a backlash, Mr Winters backpedalled from the comments and reassured staff that “the future of Standard Chartered depends on the talent, judgment, relationships and commitment of you, our colleagues.”

When asked about Mr Winters’s original “lower-value human capital” comment, Mr Dimon said: “It was an inartful way to say something, but I also think it will be all jobs. I don’t think it will be higher level or lower level, but I think it will be more than you think.”

Mr Dimon said some JP Morgan staff would be retrained and redeployed in the wake of the AI revolution, but signalled that not everyone would survive the change.

“We are going to give them reskilling, new skills, better jobs, move them somewhere else, maybe early retirement,” he said.

Forecasts vary widely in how many jobs will ultimately be replaced by AI. 

However, Dario Amodei, the chief executive of Anthropic, has warned that AI could eliminate up to half of entry-level white-collar jobs across fields such as law, finance and consulting. This could lead to the US unemployment rate rising to 20pc within the next few years.

The effect of AI may already be playing out in Britain’s job market. Graduate and entry-level roles in Britain are at historic lows, with young people struggling to find starter jobs in industries such as financial services.

data.title: Labour’s plans for British Steel are a repeat of past mistakes
data.description:
Is Britain back in the 1970s? That’s the question many ask themselves as a Labour Government prepares, once again, to re-nationalise British Steel.

Take even a cursory look at the bond market, freshly empowered unions, and the Treasury floating plans for supermarket price controls, and you could be forgiven for expecting to find yourself sipping a pint of Double Diamond while Tony Blackburn presents Top of the Pops.

As Kemi Badenoch said at PMQs this week, it’s like the socialists won. If Britain is doomed to relearn the lessons of the 1970s, then Conservatives will not make the same mistakes. That is why we cannot support Labour’s nationalisation, and we will vote against the Government’s blank cheque bill.

To be clear, this is not because we don’t think steel matters. Making our own steel is essential, just as reversing Britain’s recent deindustrialisation is. But nationalisation will not do this, because Governments have a terrible record in running businesses.

For this particular business, Labour’s plans may mean that the last of the steel made in Scunthorpe’s blast furnaces is for the nails in its own coffin.

As much as they may be to Rachel Reeves, Britain’s economic woes are not some great mystery. Steel’s problems are down to policy choices imposed on business by the Government.

Exorbitant energy prices, expensive hiring and punitive taxes. The idea that these policies could be negated by the same Labour ministers who imposed them is about as likely as Sir Keir Starmer taking up country sports.

When the Government last ran British Steel in the late 1970s, its losses infamously hit as much as £1.3bn per year. Since Labour’s soft-core, botched nationalisation of the Scunthorpe plant last year, it has already had to spend £484m – or £1.3m a day – to keep the lights on. The “economic prison” of state control, as Norman Tebbit once identified, is very expensive indeed.

It’s also likely that ministers will tilt the pitch. Faced with enormous costs – and knowing that they have the power to restrict competition – British Steel will quickly be favoured instead of every other business in the country. Exactly this has already begun.

With a flurry of Trump-style measures that double steel tariffs and cut quotas (including on steel types we don’t even make), the Government has elevated the interests of one firm over the automotive, aerospace, advanced manufacturing and defence sectors.

Even other steel firms, which import low-value steel to export high-value finished steel, will be hit.

Labour is picking one winner and making losers of everyone else. It’s no surprise that the legislation the Government has put forward includes no provision for a proper value-for-money test before a steel business is nationalised.

Instead, its sweeping powers are subject to a “public interest test” broader than Denis Healey’s bushy eyebrows. Its supposed sunset clause can be extended indefinitely.

To cap it all off, it has the very sort of Henry VIII powers to amend or disapply existing laws, contracts or common law, which Starmer once lamented in opposition.

As a socialist fog worthy of Tony Benn’s ever-present pipe descends on Whitehall, we should also not forget the unpleasant truth about Scunthorpe’s blast furnaces: Labour wants to shut them down.

Despite their nationalisation plans, ministers are holding fast to their steel strategy, which will see the blast furnaces they claim to want to save switched off in favour of more limited electric arc furnaces.

We already know this Government struggles to negotiate with others. It’s how, even after Starmer’s kowtowing expedition to Xi Jinping, and the approval of a London mega-embassy “spy base”, they still found themselves receiving nothing in return from the Chinese state, which more or less controls British Steel’s owner Jingye.

What the eventual end of the blast furnaces, even after nationalisation, will show is that this Government can’t even negotiate with itself.

The Conservatives would stop the net zero madness that has meant it makes more sense to import coking coal from Japan than to extract it here in Britain.

We will bring down energy prices by repealing the Climate Change Act, making it easier to build nuclear power stations, scrapping levies and extracting our own oil and gas from the North Sea.

To truly thrive, British Steel needs a buyer, not a Bennite solution. It was socialist stalwart Tony Benn who, in the 1970s, wanted to nationalise everything that moves – and much that had long stopped moving. The immediate result was dilapidated factories that simultaneously made bicycles and orange juice.

The end result was a bankrupt British state that had to go cap in hand to the International Monetary Fund. Labour and other parties may want to repeat those mistakes, but we won’t.

Some of these home truths may not be immediately popular. In the past, Conservatives were happy to do what was popular in the moment, only to feel the pain down the road.

Under Badenoch’s leadership, we have been clear: we will tell the truth and do what is right. This bill is not right. The truth is that we should not be nationalising British Steel.

Conservatives demonstrated that there are other options when we kept Port Talbot in Wales open by negotiating a deal with the private sector. Conservatives will fix the fundamental problems that have hurt the steel sector and manufacturing alike.

We will not pit them against each other as Labour are intent on doing, and we will not sit idly by for a rerun of the 1970s horror show Labour made Britain sit through the first time round.

data.title: Legal row looms after 800 new London homes blocked
data.description:
A major developer is preparing to mount a legal challenge after its proposals to build hundreds of new homes in south London were blocked.

Berkeley Group is considering seeking a judicial review in the High Court after the Planning Inspectorate rejected its plans to convert a shopping centre in Peckham into 867 new homes.

In its decision published on Monday, the planning inspector Matthew Shrigley called the project “overly domineering” and “visually intrusive”, warning it would obstruct views of Peckham clock tower.

Mr Shrigley ruled that the harm to heritage buildings in the area outweighed the benefits of the development, even as he acknowledged the “critical need” to build more houses in the capital.

However, Rob Perrins, the executive chairman of Berkeley Group, said the decision demonstrated “the extreme uncertainty developers continue to experience within the planning system”.

Mr Perrins said: “Housing delivery in London is at an all-time low, the Government has set clear pro‑housing policy, yet we are still blocked from building homes on a fully allocated brownfield regeneration site in a town centre.

“There is something fundamentally wrong when the inspector supports our affordable housing offer and wider benefits, recognises the acute local housing need, yet blocks development due to subjective heritage impacts, which we believe are of low harm.

“This is why developers, including Berkeley, can no longer invest in new London sites and the housing crisis continues to deepen. Decisions that block housing delivery and growth must be challenged, and we will now consider all options open to us.”

The FTSE 100 housebuilder acquired the Aylesham Centre site in 2021 and began drawing up plans to convert it into new homes.

The company said its proposals would create nearly 1,000 jobs and boost the local economy with more than £13m in additional spending.

But the plans were met with fierce opposition from local councillors and residents and campaign groups, who fundraised more than £55,000 to mount a legal challenge.

The housebuilder submitted its planning application to Southwark council, which until May’s elections was dominated by Labour, in July 2024. It lodged an appeal with the planning inspector for non-determination the following year after a decision had still not been reached.

Southwark’s planning committee then voted to reject the application, citing concerns about its proposal for 12pc affordable housing and how it would affect the local conservation area.

Sarah King, the Labour leader of Southwark council, said the inspector’s verdict marked a “great day for Peckham”.

She added: “I’d like to personally thank the community who spoke loud and clearly about their concerns. We shared those concerns and strongly argued at the public inquiry that the scheme was poorly designed and our position has been vindicated.”

data.title: Gary Lineker expands media empire with YouTube channel investments
data.description:
Gary Lineker is expanding his media empire with the launch of a new venture capital arm that will plough money into popular YouTube channels.

Goalhanger, the podcast company co-founded by the former England footballer, on Tuesday unveiled an equity investment in Invisible Media, which is behind the economics and geopolitics channel The Invisible Hand, as well as a commercial partnership with cricket creator brand Backyard Cricket.

The launch of Goalhanger Ventures marks the latest expansion of Lineker’s digital media empire, which has cashed in on the success of its podcast franchises, including The Rest Is History and The Rest Is Politics.

It suggests Goalhanger is now setting its sights on start-up media businesses as YouTube continues to scoop up audiences.

Invisible Media, which was founded by Charlie Tymon, 26, has built a large following among young audiences for its explainer videos covering economics, business, geopolitics and culture.

Its flagship channel, The Invisible Hand, breaks down topics ranging from global trade and monetary policy to power politics and corporate strategy in popular “video essays”, typically running for around 20 minutes.

Since launching in 2023, it has amassed more than 250,000 YouTube subscribers and over 35 million views.

Goalhanger said its investment would support the company’s next phase of growth, including specialist formats such as The Invisible Game, which examines the hidden economics behind everyday decisions and industries.

Going to bat

Mr Lineker’s company has also struck a partnership with Backyard Cricket, the social media brand created by Yorkshire brothers James and Mark Wood.

Beginning with informal cricket challenges filmed in their family garden during lockdown, the pair have since built a global audience through trick shots, match simulations, comedy sketches and collaborations with professional players.

Backyard Cricket has generated more than 165 million views and attracted hundreds of thousands of followers across social media platforms.

Under the agreement, Goalhanger will provide funding and strategic support spanning production, sponsorship, merchandise and longer-form video projects, while editorial control remains with the creators.

Goalhanger’s move into venture capital comes months after the company launched an incubator programme providing investment, mentoring and commercial support to emerging digital talent.

It also follows the company’s first external funding deal. In January, Goalhanger sold a minority stake to The Chernin Group, a Los Angeles-based investment firm founded by Hollywood mogul Peter Chernin.

Goalhanger, founded by Tony Pastor and Jack Davenport alongside Mr Lineker, has grown rapidly on the back of booming demand for podcasts and live events.

More than 250,000 paying subscribers now generate an estimated £15m a year in membership revenues, while the network recorded over 750 million episode views and streams last year.

Bosses have said they are targeting further expansion in the US while developing intellectual property across television, publishing, merchandising and creator-led media brands.

Mr Davenport said: “Goalhanger Ventures is about giving exceptional creator-led businesses the infrastructure to grow without losing what made them special in the first place.

“Invisible Media and Backyard Cricket are very different propositions, but they both have that rare combination of editorial clarity, audience trust and genuine momentum.”

He added: “Our role is to help them scale thoughtfully, commercially and creatively, while protecting the independence, personality and quality that their communities already respond to.”

data.title: The ‘squalid’ shopping centre holding up hundreds of new homes
data.description:
A crumbling south London shopping centre with a leaking roof and “pigeons everywhere” has become a monument to Britain’s housing crisis.

The sweeping plans to transform Peckham’s Aylesham Centre into hundreds of flats were seemingly a no-brainer for developers.

The scheme would have turned the tired 1980s shopping mall into 867 homes at a time when “housing delivery in London is at an all-time low [and] the Government has set clear pro-housing policy”, developers at Berkeley argued.

However, this week, government planning inspectors said they felt differently. In a damning ruling, the planning inspector said the new home project would “not tie in successfully with surrounding historic buildings”. The scheme was blocked on heritage grounds.

“The inspector clearly values heritage impacts over housing delivery, and this position would make development impossible in just about any town centre,” says Rob Perrins, the executive chairman of the Berkeley Group. “How can we be allowed to build next to world heritage assets like Tower Bridge, but not here?

“This is why developers, including Berkeley, can no longer invest in new London sites and the housing crisis continues to deepen.”

For Perrins, it is galling that such schemes – slated for neglected brownfield sites – are being blocked.

“If we’re no longer permitted to meet housing needs on brownfield land, then where should we build?” he asks. “It would take hundreds of acres of green belt to deliver this many homes.”

Councillors and campaign groups hailed the decision as a major win for the local area. Sarah King, the Labour leader for Southwark council, hailed it as a “great day for Peckham”.

Yet Ben Hopkinson, the head of housing and infrastructure at the Centre for Policy Studies, says he would not be “so quick to celebrate”.

“The shopping centre has zero affordable homes, so you’ve lost a place for around 80 families to have social housing.”

Meanwhile, some locals say it is not clear why such effort is being made to preserve the shopping centre.

Sam Bowman, a local resident, wrote on social media that it was “baffling that some people think the squalid Rye Lane ... is something to try to preserve instead of trying to bring up to the standard of its surroundings”.

The domed shopping arcade running through the centre is largely empty, bar a handful of stall traders, a fruit and vegetable shop and outlets selling luggage and furniture. Mould dots the bright blue meshed fabric suspended above their heads.

One stall trader says conditions in the centre have become dire. “There’s water coming out of everywhere,” he says. “The problem is the roof will never be repaired. Pigeons are everywhere.”

Campaigners claim they were justified in opposing the scheme. Aylesham Community Action says the plan would have been “gentrification [on] steroids had it been approved, displacing communities, local traders, and wrecking Peckham’s heritage”.

However, the state of the shopping centre has sparked warnings that well-intentioned opposition to development could leave communities worse off than before.

“Heritage is the reason the planning inspectorate said it was worth knocking it back, because [Peckham’s] Rye Lane is a conservation area,” says Sam Dumitriu, the head of policy at Britain Remade. “But if you’ve been to Rye Lane, it’s not your typical vision of a conservation area.

“This disused shopping centre could be turned into houses, but won’t be turned into houses. As a result, Londoners will be paying higher rents. You can’t afford to live in London because of thousands of decisions like this over decades.”

Those opposing the scheme say they are seeking an alternative development which would offer more affordable housing.

Dumitriu says that this is unlikely to yield results. Even with just 77 affordable homes out of 867, the shopping centre redevelopment was barely financially viable. Demanding more would make it impossible to build at all.

In his view, the deeper problem is a fundamental misunderstanding of how the housing market works. Homes priced at market rates trigger a chain reaction that ultimately frees up cheaper properties.

A study reviewed by the Greater London Authority in 2023 shows that in Helsinki, for every 100 market-rate homes that were built, just over 30 were freed up in the poorest neighbourhoods.

“It’s the 800 or so homes that will do more good for affordability than the 77 affordable homes,” says Dumitriu.

Still, there are some locals who say they are relieved the project is not going ahead, citing the proposed height of the tower blocks, up to 20 storeys high.

Sam Hollande, the head chef at Forza Wine, a rooftop bar nearby, says he thinks the development would have been an “eyesore” blocking views of London’s skyline, which many businesses in the area rely on.

“It’s nice there won’t be a 20-storey building towering over us like the Eye of Sauron,” he says. He adds that any development in the area should prioritise social housing and community spaces over commercial interests.

Experts say the situation is often not as clear-cut, with developers under pressure to build higher to cope with spiralling costs. This then means their developments are more likely to be rejected on heritage or design grounds.

“In the past five years, we’ve seen build costs go through the roof, so there’s just simply not enough uplift to cover affordability requirements,” says Hopkinson.

“That makes it significantly harder for developers to make the sums add up, so that’s why you saw Berkeley propose a lower [affordable homes] figure.

“To actually make the project viable, you need to build at density, so that’s your towers up to about 20 storeys, but that in turn impacted the Rye Lane conservation area. So house construction right now is between a rock and a hard place.”

Construction started on just 2,103 private new-build homes in the three months to March, findings from Molior showed in April. This compares with the 22,000 needed each quarter for Sir Sadiq Khan to meet his goal to build 88,000 homes per year.

This is being partly driven by increasing red tape, which is adding new burdens to developers submitting planning applications.

Michael Hill, a policy researcher, has pulled together provisional data showing that Berkeley’s planning application for the Peckham shopping centre far exceeds the page count of Leo Tolstoy’s War and Peace.

“It’s become really hard to build in London, and you’ve seen the number of homes being built almost entirely collapse,” Dumitriu says.

He says the Aylesham Centre project is a particularly revealing example of the challenges within Labour, which is split on the benefits of development: a Labour-run council blocking a major housebuilding scheme, contrasting with a Labour Government elected on a pledge to get Britain building again.

“Labour are unlikely to outbuild the last [government], let alone build 1.5 million homes,” he says.

Policy experts say this week’s Peckham decision will have wider implications, preventing more much-needed housing from being built.

“This shopping centre is past its best days and could have been regenerated into a lively new town centre, but that’s not going to happen anymore – it chills regeneration projects across the city,” says Hopkinson.

“If you’re a developer and you see what’s happened to this scheme, why would you go through all the hurdles of assembling land and submitting a planning application, going through consultation after consultation, complying with the litany of 500-plus regulations within the London Plan, just see it rejected?”

Berkeley has said it would consider its options, which include appealing the inspector’s decision. In the meantime, the shopping centre will remain as it has been – leaky, neglected and devoid of homes.

data.title: Labour draws up plans to switch off terrestrial TV
data.description:
Ministers are drawing up plans to switch off terrestrial TV despite concerns that it risks leaving vulnerable and older audiences behind.

Labour is understood to be supportive of plans to phase out traditional TV signals and move entirely to streaming. Government approval will be contingent on a number of conditions to ensure the move does not harm audiences.

These include ensuring that superfast broadband connections are available across the whole of the UK at affordable prices.

Ministers will also demand that streaming TV interfaces are simple and easy to use for all audiences, while broadcasters and internet providers will be required to provide help and support for those making the transition.

The conditions are expected to be outlined in a green paper, which will be published in the coming weeks. The proposals have already been signed off by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, and Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary.

Discussions over the future of terrestrial TV have proved to be politically fraught in recent years.

Broadcasters including the BBC, ITV and Channel 4 are keen to push ahead with the transition, arguing that they face huge costs to maintain energy-intensive terrestrial signals as viewers increasingly shift to streaming.

Under current legislation, traditional TV is slated to continue until at least 2034. However, campaigners have called for the deadline to be extended to at least 2040, warning that older and vulnerable audiences risk being cut off.

The campaign is backed by Arqiva, the company behind Britain’s terrestrial TV and radio masts that has a significant commercial interest in extending the life of traditional broadcast networks.

The UK’s public service broadcasters have already begun laying the groundwork for the switch-off through their Freely joint venture, which offers live and on-demand programming via streaming and is expected to replace Freeview.

The service was initially only available on smart TVs, but the broadcasters have now rolled out streaming sticks that can be plugged directly into older models that do not connect to the internet.

The move to a streaming-only future is heavily reliant on the rollout of full-fibre broadband services across the UK by providers including BT. Ministers have said they are committed to achieving nationwide coverage by 2032.

However, the Government has also raised concerns about the one million homes estimated to be struggling to afford broadband, even on cheaper “social” tariffs. Ministers are exploring a subsidy for these households, either through government support or a levy on consumer bills.

The broadcasters are teaming up with telecoms companies, as well as high street banks including HSBC, NatWest and Barclays, to argue that the digital switchover will deliver wider benefits beyond TV.

The Government has also launched a separate review into whether FM radio signals should also be switched off in the 2030s after the commercial radio industry warned it was being overlooked in discussions about switching off terrestrial masts.

A spokesman for the Department of Digital, Culture, Media and Sport said: “We are committed to ensuring that no one is left behind as TV viewing increasingly moves to online platforms.

“That is why the Government is currently working across the TV sector on a long-term sustainable approach to TV distribution in the UK. This will include a decision, as soon as possible, on whether to extend the current commitment to digital terrestrial television beyond 2034.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Russian threat forces Sweden to rethink cashless society
data.description:
Sweden has passed a law forcing food shops and pharmacists to accept cash payments, as the world’s pioneer of cashless banking rethinks its reliance on digital systems amid fears of Russian sabotage.

The legislation, approved by the Swedish parliament on Wednesday, will require food shops and chemists to provide staffed cash tills that accept coins and banknotes.

Banks will also be obliged to accept cash deposits and offer services for businesses wishing to deposit daily takings.

The move underlines how far Sweden has travelled towards becoming a fully cashless society.

Alongside neighbouring Norway, the Nordic nation has one of the world’s lowest levels of cash circulation, with consumers overwhelmingly favouring card and smartphone payments.

Shops, cafés and restaurants routinely refuse cash, while many bank branches no longer handle physical money at all.

Dependency on digital infrastructure

Before Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, Sweden was widely regarded as a model for digital payments.

Several major banks joined forces to create Swish, a mobile payment platform that allows users to transfer money instantly between accounts.

However, since 2022 and the rise in Russian hybrid warfare tactics, Swedish authorities have begun to ask whether their dependency on digital infrastructure has gone too far.

The country’s central bank has repeatedly warned about the dangers of relying on payment systems that require a constant internet connection.

“Cash is needed to enable everyone to pay and to provide an additional means of payment in the event of crisis or war,” the Riksbank said in a report published last spring.

The report also highlighted concerns that older Swedes, who still prefer to pay with notes and coins, were increasingly being shut out by shops and services refusing to accept cash.

Ulf Kristersson, the Swedish prime minister, has also warned that Sweden faces threats not only from conventional military force, but from “computers, money, disinformation and the risk of sabotage”.

Those concerns appeared to be vindicated in April last year when a major cyber attack targeting BankID, Sweden’s digital identification system, caused hours of disruption and left users unable to make payments through Swish.

Swedes urged to keep cash at home

Government pamphlets distributed to every Swedish household advising them on how to prepare for war now recommend keeping cash in various denominations at home.

In March, the Riksbank advised households to keep at least 1,000 Swedish kronor (£80) in cash to ensure they could pay for essential goods for a week during a crisis.

Swedish authorities have also worked with financial institutions to develop offline card-payment systems capable of acting as backup during a longer internet shutdown.

Cashless-payment adoption has been far from universal across continental Europe. 

In German-speaking countries, where privacy concerns remain deeply ingrained, cash is still widely viewed as a safeguard against state overreach. 

Austria has even moved to enshrine the right to use cash in its constitution, with supporters arguing that physical money provides a barrier against financial monitoring by governments and tech firms.

data.title: How to cut the cost of a granny annexe – and boost your house price
data.description:
The dour granny flat of the 1970s and 1980s is a thing of the past. Now, these trendy annexes may still be home to granny or a “boomerang” child, or be let out on Airbnb for extra income.

What’s more, adding one to your property could have a positive effect on your house price.

However, building an annexe can cost a hefty sum and there are tricky planning rules to navigate, as well as implications for your council tax bill.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how to get the annexe you want without paying through the nose.

Granny annexes aren’t just for grannies

The driving force behind the rise of granny annexes has always been multi-generational living.

These self-contained dwellings usually come with their own bathroom and kitchen (or kitchenette), as well as a bedroom and living area. As such, they are perfectly suited as homes for older relatives who perhaps need a little extra help – but don’t need to resort to the eye-watering fees that come with a residential care home – or perhaps a young adult child who would struggle to afford to rent.

As well as being more affordable than living somewhere separately, these set-ups can also offer more independence than simply having a room in the main home.

Paddy Pritchard-Gordon, of Prime Purchase, a buying agency, said: “More and more we are seeing two or three generations living together. An annexe gives everyone some welcome breathing space.”

Separately, annexes can also offer a welcome solution to those who work from home.

Mr Pritchard-Gordon added: “We are seeing a lot of people using the space as an office. For example, for those in their 20s who work remotely some of the time, it makes sense to do so from an annexe in their parents’ garden, rather than from a crowded kitchen table in a tiny rental flat.”

And the list of potential uses doesn’t end there.

Bruce King, the director at estate agent Cheffins, said: “Some like the idea of renting an annexe through Airbnb to help pay off a mortgage. I have also recently seen a real demand for annexes from London leavers, those moving to the country, who want to have an excellent space for those times when friends from out of town come to stay.”

How much does a granny annexe cost?

As these dwellings are effectively self-contained homes, complete with kitchens, bathrooms and a living space, they don’t come cheap.

As a guide, you should expect to pay between £80,000 and £100,000, according to Checkatrade – though costs can vary considerably depending on the size, design, layout, specification and location of the build.

If groundworks (trenches dug out for foundations, water pipes etc) are needed, this can bump up the price tag, as can extra features such as high-end interiors.

If you’re looking to keep costs down, you might consider converting a garage or existing outbuilding instead.

Angela Kerr, the director of the HomeOwners Alliance, said: “This could shave a significant amount off your budget. Alternatively, buying a prefabricated granny flat should also cut costs significantly.”

You can get flat-pack kits with double glazing, insulation and numbered panels from around £20,000, according to Hully Pods, a firm that offers prefab pods starting at just under £5,000.

Ms Kerr added: “Think about keeping the granny annexe near your main building. Over 10m away and it’s unlikely you’ll be able to connect to your mains.”

Having to connect utilities for an annexe that’s distant from the house is another sure-fire way to see costs escalate.

What are the rules on planning permission?

There will be different requirements for this, based on how – and where – you plan to build your granny annexe.

Kennedy Durrant, the director of Cheffins’ planning department, said: “Every council has its own policy in terms of how annexes are treated, and some are more onerous than others. Some councils ask for a ‘Section 106 agreement’, which states the building won’t be used as a separate dwelling and sold or let on the open market.”

Be sure to seek advice and check with your local planning office before pressing ahead with an application.

Mr Durrant added: “Some councils tend to like the annexe to also be in keeping with the surrounding property, preferring the conversion of a small outbuilding or barn. Equally, if it’s a brand-new construction, it’s worth trying to keep it ‘aesthetically similar’ to the main house.”

Note that some granny annexes may fall under “permitted development rights”, meaning that formal planning permission is not required.

Mr Durrant said: “If you haven’t had your permitted development rights removed [which may happen if you’re in a conservation area, for example], you may be able to put up an outbuilding in your property without planning [permission]. Sometimes, doing this first and then converting that building into an annexe can be an easier route.”

The key point is that the development must be subordinate to the main property. You need to make it clear that you have no intention of trying to build a separate house and sell it off.

Mr Durrant said: “As long as you aren’t doing anything silly, in general most councils will be likely to approve these applications.”

Mr Pritchard-Gordon added: “While every council is different, they tend to be pretty good on annexes. If you have a listed building, it can be more difficult and you will need to be sympathetic in your build. But if you’re converting an outbuilding, there doesn’t tend to be much of a problem.”

It’s also worth noting that if your granny flat is compliant with the Caravan Sites Act this can be another way to avoid the need for planning permission.

Ms Kerr said: “This is not just about caravans, but effectively the definition of a mobile home in general.”

Mobile homes and transportable buildings are treated as “caravans” for the purposes of the act – provided that they meet certain criteria.

What are the council tax implications?

An annexe may be exempt from council tax if it is self-contained and occupied by a dependent relative, such as an elderly parent or a disabled family member.

Equally, if you live in an annexe (but are not a dependent) and a relative pays council tax on the main house, you may qualify for a 50pc discount. Speak to your council about your individual circumstances.

If none of these discounts or exemptions apply, then separate council tax may need to be paid.

“This can be a negative if you are selling your property and the people buying didn’t want the annexe in the first instance,” warned Mr Pritchard-Gordon. “If you are using an architect to do the design, they will liaise with the planners and advise whether this may be an issue.”

Can a granny annexe have a positive influence on value?

Mr King said: “A smart granny flat or annexe can add some serious value in today’s market. A really great one can add around 10pc to the value of a property, assuming it is substantial and well-done, and not just an off-the-peg cabin at the end of the garden.”

Some industry estimates suggest that a granny flat can add as much as 20pc to 30pc to a home’s value – though this will, of course, depend on things such as location and build quality.

That said, bear in mind that not everyone wants – or needs – one.

Mr Pritchard-Gordon said: “A granny annexe is not usually seen as a negative, but at the same time, it might not add the value you imagine. If you think your annexe is worth £200,000, that doesn’t necessarily mean the house will sell for £X plus £200,000.

“It’s not the same as doing an extension and adding an extra bedroom or downstairs room to your property – because it’s not part of the main house.”

To maximise your annexe’s appeal, it could pay to set it up as a multi-purpose space from the very start. That way, when you come to move, prospective buyers could find it easier to imagine different ways to use the space.

Mr King said: “Perhaps fit it out so it’s ready to house granny when she needs it – or a carer, should the need arise – but in the meantime, see if it can also double up as a fantastic games room, entertaining room or Airbnb.

“Some of the best I have seen are lovely open-plan spaces, with smart and high-quality kitchenettes, vaulted ceilings and large doors on to private garden spaces.”

data.title: Hundreds of construction firms at risk of collapse, Reeves warned
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been warned that hundreds of construction firms are at risk of collapse from high taxes and surging energy costs.

Steven Mulholland, the chief executive of the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA), has written to the Chancellor to sound the alarm over the building supply chain’s future.

He said that “a significant number of firms will not survive” the increase in fuel costs caused by the Iran war, as he called for an immediate support package to reduce costs.

“For a capital-intensive, fuel-dependent sector, there is no easy substitution and no margin left to absorb what is coming,” he said.

The CPA is a trade group that represents 1,900 plant-hire companies across the UK.

Mr Mulholland said the sector has also been battered by Labour’s policies, which he equated to “death by a thousand cuts”.

“The Government’s decisions to increase National Insurance contributions, raise the minimum wage and reform inheritance tax have landed simultaneously, at pace, on firms already navigating weak pipelines and rising input costs,” he said in the letter.

“Individually, each might have been absorbed. Together, they have pushed many family firms to the edge.”

To combat the fallout from the Iran war, Sir Keir Starmer last week froze a 5p rise in fuel duty and cut fuel duty on red diesel until the end of the year, while bringing in a 12-month vehicle tax holiday for the haulage industry.

But Mr Mulholland warned that these measures will not go far enough after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz sent fuel costs soaring.

He said firms are having to cut apprenticeships and defer investment “at the precise moment” they are being asked to help deliver Labour’s target to build 1.5 million homes by 2030.

The CPA is calling for Britain to follow the likes of Ireland by introducing targeted tax relief for construction and logistics operators.

In particular, it wants Ms Reeves to temporarily cut fuel duty on commercial and off-road diesel for at least 12 months.

Leaders are also calling for VAT to be temporarily removed at the pump for fuel-intensive operators in the construction, logistics and farming industries.

Without action, Mr Mulholland said the UK risks “being the outlier” across Europe, “absorbing the competitive disadvantage while our trading partners act”.

The construction plant and equipment hire sector is worth an estimated £14bn to the UK economy and supports more than 191,500 jobs.

The warning was made after new findings by industry analyst Glenigan revealed that detailed planning approvals fell by 54pc in the year to April.

This reflected a broader drop in activity, as S&P Global UK construction purchasing managers’ index sank to 39.7 in April, down from 45.6 in the previous month.

Analysts at S&P said higher energy prices triggered by the Iran war is largely to blame. Any reading below 50 shows that a sector is contracting.

Robert Law, managing director of construction supplier AGD Equipment, said current trading conditions are the worst he has seen in years.

“All that’s happening is further cost increases,” said Mr Law. “Costs are going up, and they’re all getting passed on through the supply chain.”

A Treasury spokesman said: “We recognise the pressures facing fuel-intensive industries, which is why we have cut red diesel duty to its lowest level in more than 20 years and introduced a 12-month HGV vehicle excuse duty holiday. VAT-registered businesses can already reclaim VAT on fuel costs.”

data.title: Ministers draw up emergency powers to counter Russian cable threat
data.description:
Ministers are seeking emergency powers to protect subsea cables amid growing fears of Russian sabotage.

Liz Lloyd, the telecoms minister, said the Government was considering introducing new powers allowing ministers to force cable owners and operators to protect their infrastructure, as well as new security obligations.

The new rules would be designed to ensure that businesses were taking the necessary measures to prevent, detect and respond to security threats towards cables that are crucial for the UK’s internet access.

Under the proposals, which will be set out in a white paper later this year, ship owners and operators that recklessly damage subsea cables will also face tougher fines and prison sentences.

The proposals are aimed at strengthening Britain’s defences and deterring Russia and other hostile states amid a heightened threat of sabotage.

The Royal Navy foiled a covert operation in April by Russian submarines trying to survey critical subsea cables in the North Atlantic. More widely, there has been a 30pc increase in Russian vessels threatening UK waters in the last two years.

Speaking at the Royal United Services Institute on Friday, Ms Lloyd said: “Our message to President Putin was simple: we can see what you’re doing and any interference will have serious consequences.”

She added: “The UK already has strong protections in place for our subsea cables, but in a more uncertain world we cannot stand still.

“As hostile activity by Russia and others grows, protecting these cables matters more than ever for our economy, security and daily lives. That is why we plan to go further with tougher penalties for reckless damage, stronger security obligations and new powers to respond quickly when incidents happen.”

UK laws already carry life imprisonment for the most serious cases of sabotage clearly linked to a hostile state.

However, malicious activity under the ocean surface can be hard to spot and intent can be difficult to prove, making prosecution challenging.

Ms Lloyd said Britain’s legal system needed to keep pace with the threat, with changes planned to 140-year-old legislation designed to make the law clearer and harder to evade.

She added that the overhaul would “send a clear message that if you act recklessly, or if you deliberately target our cables, there will be serious consequences”.

There are around 60 subsea cables in UK waters, connecting Britain to the outside world. Faults are rare and the majority of incidents are accidental, with fishing activity or vessels dragging anchors the most common causes of damage.

Nevertheless, the Government has been under pressure to strengthen Britain’s defences under the sea, especially as rising data usage and the growth of AI drive up demand for more infrastructure.

In a damning report last year, MPs on the joint committee on the national security strategy found that ministers were “too timid” in defending Britain’s undersea cables.

data.title: Struggling Asda and Ocado can’t prop each other up
data.description:
Asda has announced a partnership with Ocado to expand its home delivery business. You will be able to order some competitively priced sausage rolls, get a chicken salad sandwich whisked over to your desk or even get some last-minute T-shirts from the George range if the weather stays hot over the weekend.

Sure, it is great to see two major British companies working together. The trouble is, these are two struggling businesses. Desperately trying to prop each other up is not going to work.

There is no question that investors liked the news: Ocado’s shares rose by 12pc on Friday morning. Asda will be using its software to drive its home delivery business, as well as for selling through apps such as Uber Eats, Deliveroo and Just Eat.

The tie-up will give Asda a slicker, more efficient online operation, while it will also allow Ocado to accelerate its plans to turn itself into what is essentially a software company. The deal is being spun as a win-win for both sides.

Well, perhaps. But these are two companies in deep trouble, and it is very hard to see how they can dig each other out of their respective holes.

Ever since it was taken over by the private equity firm TDR Capital and the brothers Mohsin and Zuber Issa in a highly leveraged £6.8bn takeover in 2021, Asda has been struggling. It was always way behind Sainsbury’s and Tesco in the grocery market, but now it is at risk of slipping into fourth or even fifth place. Its market share has fallen from 14.3pc before the takeover to 11.5pc now.

The German discount retailer Aldi is snapping at heels, with a 10.8pc share, and so is its great rival Lidl, which has already overtaken Morrisons to claim fifth place. On current trends, it is hard to see how anything will stop either from overtaking Asda at some point.

Likewise, Ocado is hardly in great shape either. When it started back in 2000, it was briefly one of the great hopes of the British dotcom boom, and it is, in fairness, to its credit that it has at least stayed the course when so many of its peers from that era have largely faded from view (anyone remember Friends Reunited?).

But it has constantly disappointed its shareholders over the last few years. From £24 five years ago at the peak of the pandemic-driven boom in home deliveries, Ocado’s shares have fallen all the way back to below £2 before the Asda deal.

High-tech partnerships have struggled: Kroger, in the US, announced last autumn that it was closing warehouses that used Ocado’s robots, while Sobey’s in Canada said in January that it was doing the same. It is not a very inspiring track record.

There is nothing wrong in principle with the tie-up between the two companies. It will allow Asda to improve its online offering, while it generates some extra revenues for Ocado for software and systems that it has already developed. At the margin, it will help a little. But no one should kid themselves. It is not going to turn around either business.

For one thing, Asda has already lost its way. The brand was weak to start with and it is getting weaker all the time. It is not upmarket like Waitrose and M&S, and arguably Sainsbury’s. It is not as cheap as Aldi and Lidl, nor does it even have a domestic supply chain like Morrisons. If it happens to be the closest supermarket you might shop there, but otherwise it does not have very much going for it.

At the same time, online delivery looks to have stalled at about 13pc of the total grocery market. Contrary to the bullish expectations a decade ago, or in the immediate aftermath of the pandemic, stellar growth has not been sustained and it remains a niche market. It is useful sometimes, for some people.

But most of us still prefer to push a trolley around a proper supermarket once a week instead of just browsing images of fresh fruit on our phones. There is nothing very novel about it any more, and it is very hard to see any significant growth.

Finally, online delivery is a winner-takes-all industry if ever there was one. In fairness, Asda has always done well in the online sector of the market compared to its physical stores. But as Sainsbury’s and Tesco pull further and further ahead overall, they can use that greater market share to keep prices lower, lock customers into their loyalty schemes, and beef up their brands. It is going to be a hard slog to grow online while physical retail is relentlessly losing market share.

Both Ocado and Asda are major British companies. We should hope they do well. We don’t have nearly enough flourishing homegrown businesses. It is possible that they can help each other start growing again. Who knows, perhaps over time there could be a full-scale merger.

But we have plenty of history to tell us that when two struggling companies start working together, all you get is a bigger struggling business. Attempts to prop each other up always fail – and it is hard to see that this partnership will prove the exception to that rule.

data.title: ‘My son tried to buy a plane – but Revolut lost his £384,000’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*.

Dear Ruth,

I am writing to you on behalf of my son who is currently taking flying exams in the USA. He wishes to purchase a plane in America and sent a $40,000 (£30,000) deposit using Revolut to an escrow account, which eventually landed. This was after an earlier $40,000 went missing.

He followed this up with the full balance, which he paid in pounds: £383,900. Revolut, however, changed the payment method on this amount for whatever reason and after six days, the payment was lost in the system.

Revolut keeps saying it will have an answer in two days but then says it will be a further two days. We have spoken to chatbots and senior staff, all to no avail.

The escrow time has lapsed and my son is appealing to the aviation company to hold out while he tries to secure his payment.

All this is going on while he tries to sit his exams, after which he has organised to export and fly the plane back to the UK.

– DC, Bexhill-on-Sea, East Sussex

Dear Reader,

I could tell straight away this was a very stressful time for you and your son, and of course involving a huge amount of money. 

Time was of the essence, because if the cash didn’t turn up soon, the aerospace company could, in theory, pocket your $40,000 and walk away – leaving your son without a plane and with considerable losses.

I immediately contacted Revolut explaining the urgency and to get the ball rolling on working out what had happened. I also managed to speak to your son a day later on the phone, just after he landed back in the UK.

The good news was he had passed his flying exams. The bad news was the money hadn’t arrived at its destination. This meant he had to hire a plane to do his flying exams (rather than use the one he was buying) and had to fork out for his own flight back from America. 

Your son had booked a ferry pilot to bring the aircraft home, so that was also a waste of money. This added up to thousands of pounds of unexpected costs.

Your son told me he was buying a Cirrus SR22, a small single-engine propeller plane, to rent out to pilots as part of a consortium. He’d put all his savings on the line for this purchase and new business venture.

He had been a Revolut customer for three or four months, paying for the “Metal” subscription, costing £14.99 a month. He had some business interests in China and had used Revolut to send money there, as well as to the US-based aviation company.

Revolut is one of the so-called “challenger” banks, offering slick mobile banking and money transfer services. It has been criticised in the past for its customer service, particularly around the way it has handled fraud cases and its aggressive corporate culture. 

A few months ago, it acquired a full banking licence, paving the way to launch Revolut Bank UK.

‘Lack of knowledge and expertise’

Your son said he’d had problems with the company before, when he tried to send $7,500 to China, and was very cross about its behaviour with these aeroplane payments.

However, he told me that there had been a development with the £383,900 transfer. Shortly before we spoke, Revolut had found the money and sent it back to him. But this meant he would have to try again to transfer it.

Furthermore, the original $40,000 deposit was still missing. The aviation firm said it had only received $40,000 (the second deposit your son had sent) and was missing the £383,900 balance. 

He was panicking as the company could potentially run off with the $40,000. Even if it waited for him to resend the balance, he was worried about incurring more costs due to foreign currency fees as well as the change in the £/$ exchange rate.

Your son told me: “I’m going to be hung out to dry by the exchange rate. 

“Revolut keeps saying on the ‘chat’ [on its app] that it will respond but it doesn’t. I think the company is fine for transferring less than £1,000, but for bigger amounts, its lack of knowledge and expertise becomes apparent.”

He had raised a formal complaint against Revolut before you wrote to me, and then a day after I spoke to him, he messaged me to say his account had been frozen.

This was a tricky case to get to the bottom of. Revolut assured me it was taking your son’s complaint seriously and would conduct a thorough investigation.

Firstly, it said that no block had ever been placed on the account. Secondly, the £383,900 was returned to your son because the recipient bank could not accept a SWIFT transfer, which is a type of international payment. 

I understand that large payments above £250,000 are often sent via SWIFT or CHAPS for security and liquidity reasons.

The payment was delayed by five working days, and Revolut issued a £150 goodwill gesture “in recognition of the stress this may have caused”.

Following this, your son split the £383,900 into two separate money transfers, which arrived instantly.

So, had the aerospace company waited for your son’s payments? Was he now the proud owner of a Cirrus aircraft?

Your son told me the company had demanded an extra $17,000 as he had been in breach of his contract fulfilment dates. This was highly upsetting, but he felt it was “too much to walk away from” and transferred the extra cash “to keep the sale alive”.

The purchase was finally complete, and he collected and flew the plane back to the UK.

Worryingly, though, there was still no sign of the missing $40,000. I urged Revolut to keep looking for the money.

Five weeks after you wrote to me, Revolut solved the mystery. The money had been credited to the beneficiary. Revolut says it had processed the payment correctly within 24 hours. But, when your son flagged the money hadn’t arrived, it tried to recall it. This was unsuccessful as funds cannot be recalled once credited.

It seemed the beneficiary bank was uncooperative in responding to Revolut’s requests, causing this agonising delay. It also meant your son sent another $40,000 to the aerospace firm as he thought the original payment was lost, and was concerned about the escrow deadline.

‘Ridiculous’ amount of compensation

My understanding at this point was that your son had paid about $617,107 in total for this plane. This was $57,000 more than he expected, given the $17,000 demand and the $40,000 payment he sent twice.

Thankfully, when I spoke to your son again, he said he had managed to get the first $40,000 payment back. But he remained unhappy about the other costs incurred, the “ridiculous” £150 compensation, and the responses from Revolut’s chat assistant when he was trying to find out why payments were delayed.

Revolut said: “We are very sorry to hear about [our customer’s] experience and the time taken to resolve this matter. We recognise that our initial support response regarding the status of this transaction caused confusion.”

It added that some of the delays were “due to processing constraints within the wider intermediary banking network”.

Your son says he is considering legal action against Revolut, due to the “massive amounts of money and time” this saga has cost him. 

I agree the £150 goodwill gesture is paltry. I think this is a very tough, and expensive, lesson in doing all the due diligence you can think of – and allowing ample time – before embarking on such a big purchase.

Your son also says he has switched to using HSBC for international payments, which is more expensive, but he says is more reliable. 

Finally, your son is now preparing to set up his pilot consortium, which I hope gets off to a flying start.

– Ruth

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: The battle to stop Britain burning
data.description:
Thousands of wildfires engulfed England and Wales last year, burning nearly 120,000 acres of countryside.

Homes were evacuated, crops burned and the habitat of rare birds in places including Derbyshire and Staffordshire was destroyed.

With Britain sweltering in record temperatures recently, wildfires are continuing. A fire last week broke out on the side of Arthur’s Seat, the near-wilderness in the middle of Edinburgh, with the fire service battling the blaze for two days.

Natural England, which advises the Government on nature policy, says that “virtually all wildfires” are caused by human activity, be that a cigarette flicked from a window, a portable barbecue or a piece of glass magnifying the sun’s rays to cause tinder-dry vegetation to start smouldering.

Next month sees the first all-party parliamentary group on wildfires. It will be chaired by Alison Hume, the Labour MP for Scarborough and Whitby. Her constituency was hit last August by a catastrophic fire.

But while there may be agreement on what causes wildfires, the consensus breaks down on how to prevent them.

As wildfires grow and the issue rockets up the political agenda, there is a mounting battle between environmentalists and conservationists about how to stop them.

Gamekeepers and shepherds believe the best approach is to quite literally fight fire with fire.

Traditionally, in the uplands, rural land managers have burned patches of heather and vegetation. This reduces the fuel load and limits the risk of wildfire as well as encouraging new shoots to come through for grouse and sheep.

However, the Government is increasingly opposed to this traditional practice. They argue that it has the potential to damage peat as well as creating pollution

Last year, the Government banned burning across 700,000 acres and said that burning licences had to be obtained for other areas.

The Royal Society for the Protection of Birds, one of the UK’s largest conservation charities with more than one million members, is also opposed to the practice.

“Prescribed burning simply maintains moorland in a degraded, fire-prone state, preventing it from being restored to a more fire-resilient state,” a spokesman said.

Chris Packham, a countryside campaigner and BBC presenter, is also anti-burning. He has said that if moorland were not repeatedly drained and burned “it would be a wet upland wooded habitat”.

Yet in the wilds of Britain’s countryside there is a different view.

Traditional land managers say opposition to burning may be damaging the environment and increase the risk of wild fires.

Andrew Gilruth, of the Moorland Association, which represents gamekeepers and landowners in the uplands, says that controlled burning takes place across the globe but the UK has become too risk-averse to use it properly.

It is used in the US to manage wildfire risk and is widely practised in New Zealand. It is also common in Australia, where it has been carried out by Aboriginal communities for 11,000 years.

Natural England prefers a practice known as “cutting” to burning, which relies on mowers being pulled behind tractors and “rewetting” the land.

But land managers argue that cutting still leaves a large fuel load and they also believe that rewetting is largely ineffective if a proper blaze tears through.

“We panic and want a zero risk – but not doing anything has a risk too. Other nations can see this; why can’t the UK?” says Gilruth.

Some suggest opposition to burning in the uplands is simply an assault on gamekeepers and grouse moor management.

“Nobody makes a murmur of concern if the National Trust are burning vegetation down in Dorset. Winter burning is used to reduce the wildfire risk in the New Forest by Forestry England and nobody’s got any problems.”

Natural England says 68pc of wildfires start with controlled burns that have got out of hand, although they say only 10pc of fires in the uplands have a known cause. This means little is known about what starts most conflagrations. So it would be wrong to suggest, as some people do, that 68pc of wildfires are causes by controlled burns that have become out of control.

Mr Gilruth disputes Natural England’s figures, saying that the “UK falls within international standards”.

He is not alone in supporting the burn. The National Fire Chiefs Council hit out at the Government last year when responding to its consultation on heather and grass burning, which paved the way for their ban.

“Restricting land managers’ ability to use prescribed burning as a wildfire prevention tool could compromise the fire and rescue service’s preparedness and response, increasing the danger to firefighters and the public,” the Council said.

The process of burning itself is a bit of an art.

Teams of gamekeepers and shepherds wait until the wind is blowing in the desired direction, usually away from towns and villages. After they’ve lit a fire, they use rubber paddles to beat back the flames as desired.

They will also burn buffer zones with smaller fires so that if the larger main fire later reaches them, there is no fuel and the blaze extinguishes.

Richard Bailey, a retired gamekeeper, is well versed in the intentional burning of heather on moorlands, known in the industry as a “muirburn.”

Bailey says the fire service will often send people out onto the hill with gamekeepers to learn how to manage wildfires.

“We get them coming out where we still can burn and they’ll burn with the keepers. They learn how to do it and it’s an invaluable tool,” he says. “Greater Manchester Fire Service has a tactical burns team and they have only got that because they go out with gamekeepers.”

When Bailey was a young grouse-keeper, he’d be busy in the winter and early spring carrying out “cool burns”.

“The difference between a cool and a hot burn is that with a cool burn you’re just flashing the surface vegetation off.”

Bailey says that when you burn on colder days there is more moisture in the ground.

“It doesn’t affect the mosses or the peat layer beneath. The difference with a summer wildfire is that it’s very hot, and that gets down into the root system and any exposed peat, and that’s when you start having the peat on fire.”

Peat can burn for weeks and fires often re-establish long after the fire services believe they’ve put them out.

Bailey says as well as mitigating wildfire risks, traditional controlled burning creates the perfect habitat for some of Britain’s rarest bird species.

Lapwings, for instance, which have declined by 90pc in the past 50 years, often nest on grouse moors and they like short vegetation.

With the all-party parliamentary group on wildfires starting next month, there are calls for politicians to take a more serious look at preventative measures for wildfires.

Gilruth says Hume and other rural Labour MPs now accept that the argument that controlled burning is bad “is massively oversimplified.”

A government spokesman said: “Healthy peatlands are more resilient to wildfire, support wildlife, store carbon and provide high-quality drinking water.

“We know that 80pc of the UK’s peatlands are degraded and repeated burning contributes to this, along with reducing air quality and impacting on human health.”

data.title: Broken fridges and closed counters: how Morrisons lost its northern soul
data.description:
It is 29C in Peckham, and the fridges at Morrisons have been broken for two days.

On a sticky weekday afternoon, whole aisles sit taped off. There is no fresh meat. No fish. The salad bar is closed. The butcher counter is dark.

Shoppers drift around half-empty chillers looking confused. Staff apologise wearily. Near the front of the south-east London store, one customer shrugs: “It’s always something in here lately.”

For Kate, 48, the deterioration feels symbolic of a wider decline.

“I think some of the quality has got a bit worse,” she says. “There was a period where they focused much more on the fresh stuff and the Market Street counters, but they don’t really do that any more.”

Long before Aldi and Lidl colonised Britain’s retail parks, Morrisons perfected its own kind of northern supermarket theatre: butchers in striped aprons, mountains of loose vegetables, fresh bread smells drifting across stores that felt more like indoor markets than modern grocers.

Shopping there could be chaotic, loud and faintly stressful. But it felt alive.

Now the retailer is fighting off a reputation of managed decline.

Last week, Worldpanel figures showed Lidl overtook Morrisons for the first time, pushing the Bradford chain into sixth place in Britain’s supermarket rankings.

Morrisons’ market share has slumped from 10pc in 2020 to 8.3pc today. Aldi passed it in 2022. Lidl has now followed.

Insiders argue that the supermarket had expected to slip down the rankings, with its expansion in franchised convenience stores not reflected in the market share figures.

Yet the symbolism is brutal. Morrisons once represented a distinctly British alternative to the efficiency-first discounters. Today, it straddles a dangerous middle ground, crushed by Tesco’s scale and undercut by Lidl’s prices.

“Lidl overtaking Morrisons to become the UK’s fifth-largest supermarket indicates shoppers are prioritising value and keeping costs down as households continue to feel pressure on their finances,” says Shefalee Loth, a nutritionist at Which?.

“Our monthly supermarket price analysis consistently finds that Lidl and Aldi are among the cheapest options for consumers, so it’s not surprising to see more shoppers choosing retailers where they believe they will get a cheaper basket.

“At the same time, research shows many consumers are increasingly strategic about where they shop – comparing prices more closely, making greater use of promotions and loyalty schemes, and in some cases switching supermarkets altogether.”

Morrisons is well aware of the threat. It has been among those retailers arguing for stricter rules around store openings by Aldi and Lidl, with grocery chiefs claiming this has driven the rise of the German discounters.

But part of Morrisons’ problem is financial.

Since its £10bn takeover by private equity firm Clayton, Dubilier & Rice (CD&R) in 2021, the supermarket has laboured beneath a debt pile. While Morrisons has been paying down its debt – with levels down by 46pc since the takeover – its latest financial report suggests it still stands at £3bn.

Last year it posted losses of £381m after a £281m interest bill.

The consequences are increasingly visible on shop floors.

Stores feel tired. Cafés are closing. Scores of fish and butcher counters have reduced opening hours or disappeared altogether. In May, Morrisons announced another round of store closures and job cuts as bosses warned of mounting costs.

For its loyal customers, though, something more intangible has gone missing too: the atmosphere.

“The first thing to say is that the Ken Morrison culture was quite a unique, ‘proper Yorkshire grocer’ experience,” says Clive Black, retail analyst at Shore Capital.

Under Sir Ken Morrison, the chain built its identity around fresh food counters and vertically integrated food production.

“Ken was a genius of a grocer,” says Black.

Morrisons could not out-price Tesco on tins of beans, he says, so it leaned into service, theatre and abundance instead.

“What’s happened in the intervening period,” he says, “is an industrialisation of the counter service.”

The decline has been gradual rather than sudden. During the austerity years after 2010, Morrisons cut back heavily on labour-intensive counters and fresh service.

David Potts, appointed chief executive in 2015, partially reversed course, reviving counters and improving sales.

But under the debt pressures of the CD&R era, the cost-cutting instinct has returned.

“There has been a reduction in the number of fishmongers, the number of butchers, a cut in the number of hours certain counters are open,” says Black.

Morrisons argues that its in-store bakers, butchers and fishmongers remain “the cornerstone of our offer and a critical point of differentiation for our customers”.

A spokesman says it is “completely committed to these and continues to invest in and develop them”. It is understood that no further counter closures are planned this year.

Still, Black says some of those remaining are not what they once were. “In some Morrisons stores, you’ll see pre-packed products actually on the counter. That’s all about cost management.”

It is a drive which has been overseen by former colonel and Carrefour veteran Rami Baitiéh, who was brought in to helm the business in November 2023. Sources suggest some staff have found his approach jarring.

A recent focus on boosting sales of general merchandise to rival Aldi and Lidl’s middle aisles has “cheesed people off”, says one former executive.

Headhunters claim there is a broader “frustration over Baitiéh’s working style”.

Since Baitiéh took over, the supermarket has introduced new demands for head office teams to be seen at their desks more often and for shop-floor staff to work faster.

“The turnover of people isn’t over,” one senior headhunter says.

Despite Baitiéh’s productivity drive, so far there has not been a dramatic improvement in Morrisons’ performance.

Its market share has continued to slip since the Frenchman’s arrival, although he has sought to argue that the drop since late 2023 from 8.5pc to 8.3pc is a sign it is now stemming the decline.

Still, “Rami was expecting to see more success from his approach,” the headhunter says.

It is perhaps unsurprising that Morrisons has not roared back to life. Many stores feel trapped in an uncomfortable limbo between old and new. Morrisons is racing to boost investments in refurbishments but continues to face problems within its shops.

At one London branch, imported turkey products sat beneath signs invoking British farming. In South Wales, the company was fined £737,000 after inspectors found “serious and systemic failures” at a bakery operation.

Earlier this year, Morrisons became embroiled in controversy after sacking a store manager who tackled a shoplifter.

Despite the gloom, there is a glint of hope that Morrisons retains advantages that rivals struggle to recreate.

It remains one of Britain’s most vertically integrated grocers, producing much of its own fresh food through its Myton manufacturing arm. Unlike Tesco or Sainsbury’s, it still has a recognisable service-counter proposition, even in diminished form.

A spokesman for Morrisons argues the retailer is “unique”, adding: “Our growth reflects the breadth of our operations across supermarkets, online, convenience and manufacturing.”

He also claims that Worldpanel’s figures “underestimate our true market share position,” and says, “we have maintained our share while not opening new supermarkets, unlike the discounters who continue to add significant new space.”

He adds that Morrisons bosses are making “real progress with our strategy, growing sales, improving the performance of our supermarkets [and] delivering significant growth.”

However, retail analyst Black says: “If you ask most people, they wouldn’t know that Morrisons is a vertically integrated business. That’s probably something the company should work on harder.”

Discounters have trained shoppers to prioritise price above all else, while Tesco’s Clubcard ecosystem has rewired loyalty shopping.

Labour-intensive counters look increasingly uneconomic in an industry obsessed with efficiency. But Black argues Morrisons would be making a catastrophic mistake if it abandoned them entirely.

“Morrisons’ theatre and counters are something you ultimately play with at your peril,” he says.

“If it was taken out, the reasons to go to Morrisons would be seriously questioned.”

Back in sweltering Peckham, shopper Kate, a one-time Morrisons employee, laments the supermarket’s decline.

“You’d go there because it felt different. Now, you can’t always buy meat, the counters aren’t really open and it feels like they’ve lost that,” she says.

“It’s sad, because when I used to work there it was actually a really nice place to work. It was popular. It was busy.”
