data.title: Google’s plan to run your life could break the internet
data.description:
Many people regret appearing in Playboy in their youth. Google’s two co-founders might be an unlikely addition to this group.

In 2004, Larry Page and Sergey Brin gave an extensive interview to the magazine that threatened to derail the search engine’s Nasdaq initial public offering, disrupting a pre-flotation “quiet period” in which executives are meant to stay silent.

Ultimately, the storm passed. But Page’s comments during the interview have since hung over the company.

“We want to get you out of Google and to the right place as fast as possible,” Page said.

At the time, Google was known for its “10 blue links”. The company prided itself on a slick, uncluttered interface that produced better search results than its myriad rivals, which at the time were attempting to stuff search results with their own services.

“Most portals show their own content above content elsewhere on the web,” Page said. “We feel that’s a conflict of interest, analogous to taking money for search results.”

Today, critics say this philosophy has been dropped. No Google search only features 10 blue links, and Google’s own services, from shopping to YouTube videos, feature prominently at the top of its results.

Increasingly, the company’s “AI overviews” and more detailed “AI mode”, both generated by artificial intelligence, answer questions without the user needing to leave the page.

The company took the latest step down this path last week. Google will allow users to ask follow-up questions of its AI overviews, which may further reduce the incentive to click through to websites. Search results will also allow people to build mini-apps and “agents” that can scour the web on your behalf, booking restaurants or hotel rooms on your behalf.

“We’re entering the era of search agents,” Elizabeth Reid, the head of Google search, said.

Critics believe we are approaching “Google Zero”: a future in which Google stops sending users to external websites and instead keeps them on its site indefinitely.

The features have been popular. Sundar Pichai, the chief executive of Google, said 2.5 billion people are seeing AI overviews, and a billion are using AI mode.

These features allow Google to maintain its grip on consumers’ attention and keep them away from rival AI systems such as ChatGPT and Claude from Anthropic.

“Google is leveraging its captive audience for search to keep them engaged with AI [features] to keep them on their platform longer rather than have them move over to ChatGPT or Claude,” says Gadjo Sevilla, an analyst at eMarketer. “It is pushing to be the default consumer AI through Google Search’s dominance.”

Niamh Burns, of Enders Analysis, says the latest changes mean “consumers can offload more and more of their cognitive effort, and their website visits, to AI”.

Yet there is a downside. For websites that have spent years relying on Google traffic, the changes represent a heavy blow.

In a report published earlier this year, the Reuters Institute found that traffic to news websites from Google’s search engine plunged by a third in 2025. Industry bosses said they expected traffic to fall a further 43pc in the next three years.

Johannes Beus, of search engine optimisation company Sistrix, says that appearing first in Google search results typically means users will click the link 27pc of the time. But when those results feature AI overviews, this drops to 11pc.

Beus predicts this percentage will continue to fall as more efficient software makes it less computationally intensive to deliver AI answers. “Zero-click searches will continue to expand,” he says.

‘Devastating’ effect on traffic

Reach, the owner of the Mirror, Express and dozens of regional titles whose unrelenting hunt for digital attention has triggered repeated accusations of clickbait, is among the biggest victims. In March, the company slumped to its largest loss in a decade, warning of a sharp decline in referrals from Google.

All About Berlin, an English-language online guide to the German capital, said the launch of Google’s AI overviews had had a “devastating” effect on traffic.

“It’s hard to fund my work with 70pc fewer visitors,” Nicolas Bouliane, the website’s owner, wrote on X. “In another year, it will be impossible. Instead of writing new guides, I spend my days preparing for that future.”

Theo Bamber, the chief executive of the News Media Association (NMA), which represents titles including The Telegraph, The Guardian and The Times, describes Google’s latest changes as a “significant and worrying acceleration of the integration of AI into traditional search”.

“By burying links to trusted sources of information beneath synthetic AI summaries, major platforms risk severing the vital connection between news publishers and their audiences, meaning trusted sources of information become harder to find,” he adds.

Claims of copyright

The row is not just about a decline in traffic. Web publishers accuse Google and other tech giants of using vast swathes of copyrighted material to train their models without permission.

Google has defended this, saying in legal filings: “Using publicly available information to learn is not stealing.”

Google has already locked horns with the news industry regarding its AI updates, branding publishers “free riders” as they sought to opt out of AI summaries.

This triggered a furious response from the News Media Association, which argued that Google had been free-riding by repurposing articles for AI without compensation or consent.

The industry body is now pushing the regulator to crack down on Google and “correct this imbalance”.

The Competition and Markets Authority has set out proposals that would allow publishers to choose whether or not they want to appear in AI search summaries.

The crackdown, which could come into force next year, would also ban the tech giant from “manipulating” search results by favouring big advertisers or punishing companies that have criticised it. Google denies engaging in such practices.

News is a small part of the web. But this row may only be an early sign of cracks in the symbiotic relationship between Google and the websites that feed it.

Threat of a disruptive new feature

The company’s latest features, unveiled last week, do more than just summarise information. Google’s “search agents”, a feature that will be available only to paying subscribers at first, will be able to repeatedly search the web on your behalf, for example, monitoring property websites for flats to rent or checking online shops for new trainers.

“You could be asleep, and it’s still helping you,” Robby Stein, a Google executive, told Wired.

This threatens to disrupt whole industries that rely on Google. If agents, not humans, are now going to be the ones that browse the web, anything that relies on attracting eyeballs is in trouble.

“[It] brings the broader internet’s inconvenient truth even more to the forefront than it’s already been,” wrote Brad Erickson, an analyst at RBC.

Google’s agents threaten to “transition value” away from online marketplaces such as Airbnb, Booking.com and Uber. The day after Google’s announcement, shares in Reddit – the online mega-forum that gets a large portion of traffic from the search engine – fell by 5pc.

Google has said everyone can benefit from what it sees as a growing pie. It says the number of searches is at a record high and that it is sending more “quality clicks” to websites. But web publishers are unhappy.

According to internet company Cloudflare, 8pc are choosing to block Google completely, sacrificing their appearance in search results rather than have the company’s bots scrape their sites.

If this snowballs, it could be as painful for Google as it is for the websites that rely on it.

“There needs to be a functioning economic grounding here if the open web isn’t going to wither,” says Burns. “Google does rely on this ecosystem, so this is Google’s problem to solve too.”

data.title: Tony Ridley, London Underground chief who was forced to resign after the 1987 King’s Cross fire
data.description:
Tony Ridley, who has died aged 92, created the Tyne & Wear Metro and Hong Kong’s Mass Transit Railway, and initiated the planning to cope with the near-tripling of passengers on the London Underground since the early 1980s.

But he was forced to resign as ​chief executive of LU in the wake of the King’s Cross fire of November 19 1987, which killed 31 people, being blamed for the absence of a fire safety culture within the organisation.

The Tube’s safety record was relatively good despite ageing infrastructure and arcane working practices; until the fire, which began beneath an escalator, there had – apart from the 1975 Moorgate disaster, the cause of which has never been identified – been just four passenger fatalities in 43 years. But a rigid commitment to safety on the track had not been matched elsewhere.

After hearing evidence for 91 days, in November 1988 Desmond Fennell QC concluded that a lighted match dropped on to the escalator in breach of a ban on smoking had started the fire, which flared up because of a previously unknown “trench effect”.

Fennell’s report accused manage​ment of complacency: station staff received little or no fire training, and were under instructions only to dial 999 once a blaze was out of control. Fennell blamed Ridley and Sir Keith Bright, chairman of London Regional Transport, for the culture that had allowed the disaster to happen.​

The Transport Secretary, Paul Channon, handed them copies of the report and invited them to consider their positions. Bright resigned on the spot; Ridley had to be persuaded by his staff.

His resignation was an unfortunate end to a progression of jobs in which he had demonstrated a flair for identifying demand for public transport and devising projects to meet it. Even at LU, he had not only begun catching up on a 50-year backlog of maintenance and renewals, but had helped launch the Docklands Light Railway.

Tony Melville Ridley was born in Sunderland on November 10 1933, the son of John Ridley, a mining engineer, and his wife Olive. From Durham School he took a BSc at Kings’ College, Newcastle, then postgraduate degrees at Northwestern University (Illinois), and Berkeley. A spell at Stanford followed, then after five years at the Nuclear Power Group he joined the University of California in 1962.

Three years later, the Greater London Council recruited Ridley as its highways and transportation research officer. Sir Bill Fiske, the GLC’s Labour leader, said he had “reversed the brain drain”.

In 1969 Ridley became chief executive of the newly formed Tyne & Wear Passenger Transport Executive. Councillors expected him to concentrate on merging three loss-making municipal bus undertakings, but he saw a bigger opportunity.

Newcastle possessed a commuter rail network, including a circular route to the coast, but usage had declined to the point where British Rail had replaced electric trains with diesels. Ridley proposed converting it to light rail, with bus interchanges.

Civic leaders rallied behind the scheme, and through Ridley’s Whitehall contacts secured £50 million to design and build the Metro. His enthusiasm enabled Tyneside to leapfrog other conurbations, like Manchester, with metro plans of their own.

Ridley’s successor recalled: “BR decided to pull out all the stops with a scheme of its own and presented it to the Transport Minister John Peyton one Monday morning – only to discover he had awarded us the grant the previous Friday.” Metro opened in 1980; it now has 60 stations, carries more than 100,000 passengers a day and is still expanding.

In 1974 Ridley was headhunted to take charge of Hong Kong’s MTR, on which work was about to begin. He planned for its operational and commercial success, and the first train ran in 1979. MTR became one of the world’s busiest and most profitable metros, with lucrative property developments at its stations.

Ridley left Hong Kong in March 1980 to become joint managing director of the civil engineers Halcrow. Four months later he was appointed Managing Director (Railways) at London Transport.

At Hong Kong MTR, Ridley said, “I was managing director of 3,000 people and a brand new company where every decision was a precedent. At LT the general view was: ‘Any bloody fool can build a new railway in Hong Kong, but running one 100 years old is a real man’s job.’ ”

His first board meeting was interrupted by a member saying an infinitive had been split, and demanding a contribution to a “swear box”. Ridley interjected that he would first like to hear the point being made.

He quickly grasped that the Tube was “old and tatty”, with a maintenance backlog and outdated working practices. It was also out of favour with Margaret Thatcher’s Government because of Ken Livingstone’s fare-cutting campaign. In June 1982 services were cut to save £4 million a year; deeper cuts of 12 per cent followed that autumn.

Bright took the chair of LT in September 1982; from 1985 Ridley was chairman and managing director of LU, and from 1988 chief executive. In 1984 the Government took over the undertaking from the GLC pending the council’s abolition.

Ridley unveiled plans to slim staffing over 20 years from 23,500 to 16,500, eliminate guards and introduce driverless trains. Staff struck over one-man trains on the East London Line, but they were introduced. Eight stations were proposed for closure, plus two outlying lines; the Aldwych and Ongar branches eventually went.

With passengers up 40 per cent from a historic low of 480 million in 1982 (today 1.2 billion), Ridley worked to increase Tube capacity while modernising the system. By the time the DLR – which he insisted must be managed separately – opened in 1987, planners were working on extensions for the Bakerloo, East London, Jubilee and Metropolitan lines, and the remodelling of the most congested stations.

That September he initiated a review of LU’s structure, an analysis of traffic demand and a 10-year strategy for meeting it. This concluded that there was scope for increasing capacity through central London, partly by building Crossrail (now the Elizabeth Line) – but at a price. Ridley argued: “London is competing with Paris to be the capital of Europe. The French won’t be hanging around. If we’re to win we have to have a city that works.”

Then came the King’s Cross fire, at the end of the evening rush hour. At the scene, Ridley said: “We have examined the area underneath the escalators and there was no evidence at all of what we feared – rubbish collected there.” He disputed the unions’ claims that the fire resulted from spending cuts.

At the Fennell inquiry, Ridley denied that management had been complacent over safety. It had been turning an “aged, ineffective and fairly tatty” system into one carrying a record number of passengers. He agreed that the safety of passengers in stations had not been the highest priority, then withdrew the statement.

Already a director of Eurotunnel, Ridley became its joint managing director in 1989; his brief was to oversee boring of the running tunnels beneath the Channel.

The post was a poisoned chalice: Eurotunnel’s chief executive Alastair Morton wanted Ridley to crack down on the contractors, and the contractors hoped he would cut Morton down to size. These tensions jeopardised the banks’ release of finance, and early in 1990 John Neerhout, of the American engineers Bechtel, was brought in to break the deadlock. Ridley left soon after.

In 1991 he became Rees Jeffreys Professor of Transport Engineering at Imperial College, and from 1997 to 1999 he headed Imperial’s world-class Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering; since 1999 he had been Professor Emeritus. He was also senior transport adviser to the London 2012 Olympic bid.

Ridley was international president of the Chartered Institute of Logistics and Transport from 1999 to 2001, and president at various times of the Association for Project Management, the Commonwealth Engineers’ Council, the Institution of Civil Engineers and the Light Rail Transport Association. He was a freeman of the City of London, and held numerous honorary degrees, awards and fellowships. He was appointed CBE in 1986.

​Tony Ridley married Jane Dickinson in 1959; they had two sons and a daughter. For the past seven years, he had been in a care home with dementia.

Tony Ridley, born November 10 1933, death announced April 29 2026

data.title: BrewDog founder James Watt plots comeback with new beer brand
data.description:
James Watt, the BrewDog founder, is poised to return to the beer industry two months after the collapse of the craft brewer he helped build.

The entrepreneur has unveiled plans for Second Best, a new brewery that will offer former BrewDog investors and ex-staff a free stake in the venture as he attempts to rebuild a community-owned beer business from scratch.

Announcing the plans on LinkedIn, Mr Watt said: “Thousands of people trusted me to build a brilliant beer business and create value for them. It was an obligation I took very seriously. And I, for one, am not done with that obligation.”

Under the proposal, up to 19.3pc of the company will be handed to former BrewDog “Equity Punk” investors, whose holdings were wiped out when the brewer was sold to US cannabis and drinks group Tilray Brands in a £33m rescue deal in March.

Mr Watt said: “You can claim the exact stake you once held in BrewDog, for free.

“No catches, no cash required, and your equity in Second Best will always rank alongside my own. You’ll own it. I’ll fund it. And I’ll dedicate myself to building it.”

The offer is expected to be extended to former BrewDog bar staff, almost all of whom had shares in the business at the time of the brewer’s collapse.

Mr Watt said former investors would become “Second Founders” rather than shareholders and pledged to build “a world-class beer business that we all collectively own”.

The venture has yet to secure all the licences and approvals required to launch, and no official opening date has been set.

Mr Watt, who is married to Georgia Toffolo, a Made in Chelsea star, said the company would initially unveil an “alcohol-adjacent” concept before entering the beer market.

According to the Financial Times, Second Best will focus primarily on canned beer rather than recreating the sprawling pub empire that became synonymous with BrewDog’s expansion.

Mr Watt said the business could eventually open a small number of specialist beer-focused pubs.

He told the paper: “I feel an obligation to the Equity Punk investors. I want to try to create the future of beer.

“Hopefully, the second beer business I build with the community will be the best one.”

BrewDog’s downfall

The launch marks a swift return for one of Britain’s most controversial entrepreneurs following the spectacular downfall of BrewDog.

Founded by Mr Watt and Martin Dickie in Aberdeenshire in 2007, the brewer rode the craft beer boom to become one of the industry’s biggest success stories.

At its peak, BrewDog operated more than 120 bars across 57 countries and attracted thousands of small investors through its Equity Punks crowdfunding scheme.

The company was valued at around £2bn in 2021. However, the business’s fortunes deteriorated sharply in the years that followed.

BrewDog recorded five consecutive annual losses from 2019 onwards while debts linked to its private equity backer, TSG Consumer Partners, mounted.

By the time of its collapse, those liabilities had swollen to more than £800m.

The company was also rocked by allegations from more than 60 former employees in 2021, who accused BrewDog of fostering a toxic workplace culture. Mr Watt later apologised and said management needed to “listen, learn and act”.

He stepped down as chief executive in 2024, although he remained closely associated with the business he co-founded.

BrewDog was sold to Tilray for £33m last month – a sharp collapse in value from its former multi-billion-pound status.

The transaction wiped out Equity Punk investors, TSG Consumer Partners and the shareholdings of both founders.

Nearly 500 jobs were lost after dozens of pubs were closed after the rescue.

After BrewDog’s collapse, Mr Watt wrote: “I am heartbroken for all of our brilliant equity punks who did not get the return on their investment they wanted” and “heartbroken to have dedicated the best 20 years of my life to something that ultimately did not have the ending we all wished for”.

He told The Telegraph on Friday that 500 former Equity Punks had signed up with the brand after 10 minutes, and since then more than 2,000 have registered, adding: “I am going to make a non-alcoholic beer for my non-drinking wife.”

data.title: ‘John Lewis is refusing to honour a £300 gift card I was saving for my first child’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*.

Dear Ruth,

My wife and I received John Lewis gift cards as wedding presents in 2022. Our friends generously contributed money to the retail giant in good faith in exchange for goods. We spent some of them, but recently came across two others totalling £300.

We have been unable to use them as apparently they are not valid anymore. It turns out they expire after two years.

When we found these gift cards, we also found an Ikea gift card, Amazon gift card and a Virgin Experiences voucher. The Virgin voucher had also expired but the company made an exception and gave us 14 days to redeem it. The Ikea gift card lasts for five years, so we still have time to spend it and the Amazon card is valid for 10 years.

John Lewis is clearly out of step with its competitors, which present much more amenable terms and conditions for their customers. Furthermore, the John Lewis gift card does not state the date of issue or expiry, so it’s not clear.

Through our wedding registry alone, my wife and I spent close to £5,000 with John Lewis, and we chose the company because of its longstanding reputation for fairness and customer care.

I am pregnant and expecting a baby this summer, and we had planned to use the gift cards for baby essentials, as well as to set up another John Lewis registry for the occasion.

I have phoned and emailed the retailer several times and asked for my case to be escalated. I was told by a customer service representative that gift cards can be renewed in extenuating circumstances, but I have now been told John Lewis never makes an exception and will not budge. We are so disappointed.

– OD, north London

Dear reader,

You’re not the first person – and you certainly won’t be the last – to get upset over a forgotten gift card. Many gift cards and vouchers are not spent before they expire, and retailers profit hugely from this. In fact, I can see in John Lewis’s annual report for 2026 that it has £101m worth of unredeemed gift cards on its balance sheet.

There are no rules in this country about expiry dates on gift cards. Interestingly, some countries such as Ireland and the US have seen fit to give consumers more protection, with both stating that vouchers and gift cards must be valid for at least five years. This is certainly an area where I’d urge the UK Government to take a closer look.

As you point out, some companies are more generous than John Lewis with longer expiry dates, while others are more flexible when you ask to extend them.

John Lewis is not the worst, though, as some gift cards are valid for just one year. I did a bit of digging and discovered those issued by firms including Ryanair, White Stuff, Ticketmaster, Notonthehighstreet, Costa Coffee and Vue expire after a measly 12 months; so if any readers have one of those, make sure you spend it quickly.

The issue of expired gift cards has been tackled previously in this column, with mixed results.

You have repeatedly appealed to John Lewis to consider your case with “care and compassion”. You told me that £300 is a significant amount of money to you and “it’s difficult to accept that the remaining balance can be retained without providing anything in return”.

John Lewis’s terms and conditions, which can be found on the gift card, state that a card is valid for two years from purchase and making a purchase or checking your balance refreshes the 24-month period. So, customers can restart the two-year clock by checking the balance or buying something.

Annoyingly, the actual expiry date isn’t written on the card, meaning if you’ve received it as a present, you may be completely in the dark about when the card will become worthless.

I was interested that you said you were told a gift card could be renewed in extenuating circumstances, so I asked John Lewis to look again at your case.

A few days later, you received an email from the retailer. It said gift cards are treated as cash so are not replaced if lost or expired, and this is “a policy that is rigidly adhered to without exception”.

It also explained that card balances and transactions were stored on their system and “an expiry date minimises the amount of storage required for this data”.

I spoke again to my contact at John Lewis and was met with a firm “no”.

A spokesman said: “We are really sorry to hear that these gift cards expired and we are unable to replace them. Like many retailers, our gift cards are valid for two years from purchase, however this can be extended by making a transaction or checking the balance. A gift receipt is also included so the recipient can see the card’s amount and purchase date.”

This is a tough outcome, and it’s a shame you won’t have the money to spend on baby items.

For anyone receiving a gift card, make sure you find out the expiry date and put a note in your diary. It’s best to spend it quickly before it is lost or forgotten, or in case the company goes bust.

Out of curiosity, I asked the Government whether it would consider strengthening the rules around gift cards. The Department for Business and Trade said: “We expect retailers to be clear and transparent about when their gift cards expire, but there is no legal requirement to extend expired cards.”

Businesses must not mislead consumers about gift card expiry dates (doing so could be a criminal offence), but it seems that there are no plans to follow in the footsteps of Ireland and the US by mandating a minimum expiry period.

– Ruth

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Lorry drivers are the unsung heroes of the modern economy
data.description:
For 10 years we’ve been told that the job of lorry driver – my job – is in imminent danger of automation, yet that day seems almost as far off as it’s ever been.

The number of automated lorries in the world is tiny, and none of them are at the stage where they could fully replace a human driver in all conditions. This is after billions have been invested, which must surely make it one of the worst investments in AI, considering the uptake of the technology elsewhere. Perhaps the job isn’t as simple as some thought.

People like me will need to sit in cabs for many years to come. This is especially the case in Britain, where our narrow roads don’t lend themselves to autonomous large vehicles.

The importance of freight cannot be overstated. Look around you, right now. Almost every man-made thing you can see has been on a heavy goods vehicle (HGV) at some point. Most products are the result of several journeys. That gadget from China has been on at least two container trucks and one distribution truck. The subcomponents have been on trucks, as have the raw materials, and the packaging, and the raw materials for the packaging. And so on.

The sheer scale of this hides in plain sight. A very large supermarket in Britain will need at least 10 full-sized articulated lorries to keep it resupplied per day. That’s just one supermarket, and there are thousands. All this leads to a simple yet sobering equation: no trucks, no economy. Without lorries, manufacturing stops, imports stop, farming stops. Watch Clarkson’s Farm to see how important HGVs are for getting that most basic of needs – food – to us.

But beyond getting everyday goods to consumers, trucking often intersects with major news stories.

Right now, we sit on the cusp of a supply-chain crisis due to events in Iran. My Middle Eastern lorry-driving brothers are being called on to shift containers overland to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. Here, the 110-year-old truck-and-semi-trailer design yet again proves to be the most versatile and necessary component of the modern supply chain, along with the people who drive them.

During the Covid lockdowns, we were suddenly elevated to being “key workers”, with members of the public clapping in gratitude from motorway bridges. The truth is that we are key workers all of the time. And now the gratitude seems to have gone, we’ve returned to being necessary but forgotten.

So what kind of men and women do we need for the job, why do we do it?

Well, for one, being at the logistical frontline allows us to see things for how they really are. Think of Brexit and the constant prophecies of doom about the effect on cross-border trade.

I can report that it did create extra work, but for the most part it was easily dealt with. Those German customs officers in Aachen could have you processed in 15 minutes, France was usually even simpler, other countries could be an hour or two.

But Brexit was predictable, so the industry could adjust. There are plenty of other potential impediments to road transport that aren’t. French industrial action, bad weather in the Channel, an accident at the Dartford River Crossing; these could all cause massive delays, yet no one seems at all concerned about their effects on trade.

But beyond that, the job is so very different from the average office job. Drivers are like soldiers in that if they aren’t complaining, they aren’t happy, but I’ll confess to aspects I really enjoy. There are very few jobs that allow you space to think about what you want to think about. And to listen, all day, every day. I consume audiobooks and podcasts relentlessly. Then there are the ever-changing vistas to be seen from my “office”, with its elevated and panoramic views of the countryside. There is also a strong camaraderie between drivers.

You have to be comfortable with working alone and independently. When I did Euro work, a typical Monday morning briefing would be “OK, you’ve got five drops in Germany. Clear customs in Aachen tomorrow, last drop Munich by 3pm Thursday, text us when you’ve done”. That would be it. Ferries, routes and overnight stops would be up to me, and unless something went wrong, I wouldn’t talk to anyone from the office for four days.

There are drawbacks. There’s a fine line between solitude and loneliness. I’ve struggled to form relationships since coming back to the job. Some problems – many in fact – are due to outside forces. The parlous state of the roads in Britain weighs more on us than on most.

For most, a few potholes on the way to work is a hassle. Having to deal with them for up to 10 hours a day has a significant effect on fatigue. The industry and drivers have long complained about the lack of good parking facilities in this country, but the government has constantly allowed the situation to get worse. Beyond all else, I think it would be nice to get a little more acknowledgement, an understanding that our essential role is remembered.

Graham Brown is a lorry driver and writer. You can find his Substack here

data.title: Obsession with speed blamed for HS2 failure
data.description:
The obsession with HS2’s speed is to blame for its failure, an official review has found.

The project’s focus on achieving the “highest possible speeds” has led to spiralling costs that have crippled progress, according to Sir Stephen Lovegrove.

The former national security adviser said the high-speed rail line had gone “disastrously wrong” because of the decision to “gold plate” the project and political pressure to “keep things moving”.

HS2 was originally intended to run at 223mph, about 100mph faster than the country’s existing trains, which would have made it one of the fastest in the world.

However, on Tuesday, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, is expected to confirm that the speed for the line will be reduced to 198mph to reduce costs.

This is because the higher speeds cannot be tested without building a bespoke new track.

HS2 was originally designed to run between London, Birmingham, Manchester and Leeds and cost £32bn.

However, the project will only run as far as Birmingham on a new track, before connecting to the West Coast Main Line.

Latest estimates suggested that this line could cost £100bn, the equivalent of £1bn a mile, The Times reported.

Sir Stephen was commissioned by Sir Keir Starmer to look at the HS2 project.

Pressure from ministers

In his report, which is set to be published on Tuesday, he concluded that there was a discrepancy between the “mission” of ushering in a “new age of high speed rail” and the task of delivering “within time and budget”.

He said: “The role of politics has been repeatedly raised by interviewees and previous reviews. This is an important subject and there is little doubt that all players, certainly at HS2 Ltd and the department, felt under significant pressure from ministers to keep things moving.”

Sir Stephen’s review also highlighted several “original sins”, with one being “the original ‘gold plating’ of the high-speed concept ... resulting in bespoke and highly engineered design and the decision to begin construction at the hardest points of the route”.

He said the “changing objectives and political priorities” had resulted in estimates of the project’s costs being “disastrously wrong”.

He also highlighted a report by James Stewart, a former KPMG infrastructure advisory chairman, who said politicians were so scared that HS2 would be cancelled that they allowed costs to spiral out of control.

The project is now being led by Mark Wild, who was recently named as Britain’s highest-paid public servant, earning four times the salary of the Prime Minister.

Mr Wild, who became HS2’s chief executive in December 2024, earns between £660,000 and £665,000 a year, according to figures published by the Cabinet Office in March.

His salary was described by campaigners as a slap in the face for taxpayers given the fact plans to begin running services between London and Birmingham by 2033 “cannot be achieved”.

Ministers are also rewriting a £2bn contract for trains that are the incorrect length for some platforms along the proposed route.

data.title: HR is a soul-destroying racket. We’re about to see definitive proof
data.description:
You can imagine the anguish among HR “practitioners” when they heard the news that the American financial technology start-up Bolt had fired its entire human resources department. Surely the business is now collapsing?

Presumably, productivity has slumped and customer satisfaction is in freefall. What will Bolt do when all its “social responsibility” and “diversity” targets are missed next quarter?

The awkward truth for HR is that, according to Bolt’s chief executive, Ryan Breslow,, the company is actually thriving. “We had an HR team, and that HR team was creating problems that didn’t exist,” Breslow said. “Those problems disappeared when I let them go.”

Bolt, which makes software to speed up online checkouts, has been through a rough few years. When Breslow, its founder, returned to the business to turn it around, he fired a third of the workforce, including the entire HR department. He had worked out that HR was draining the life out of what was meant to be a fast-moving business. HR had the “wrong energy, format, and approach”, he argued. By contrast, the handful of staff in the “people ops” team who replaced them “empowers managers, streamlines decision making, and keeps the company moving at lightning speed”.

It is only one example of course. But it is possible that Breslow has just called time on the entire HR racket.

The “people profession” as it grandly likes to describe itself, has long since moved on from the days when it used to issue the payslips and organise the holiday rota. It is now engaged in championing “diversity”, caring for “mental health”, and maintaining “team spirit”, mostly by drawing up endless codes, and arranging away-days at four-star hotels conveniently close to a motorway.

It has certainly created a lot of work for anyone who has mastered the right mix of jargon, virtue-signalling and hectoring that is the main qualification for a flourishing career in HR. Over the last 15 years, the number of HR officers in Britain has grown by 83 per cent, compared with a 13.5 per cent rise in the overall number of people employed. They now make up nearly 1.5 per cent of the entire workforce, more than doctors, lawyers or police officers.

Chief people officer, according to an analysis of LinkedIn, the social network where they like to hang out, was until recently one of the fastest-growing job titles, closely followed by a key member of their team, the “diversity and inclusion” manager.

In fairness some of that is necessary. Successive governments, with the current one guiltier than most, keep passing more and more employment legislation. Someone has to keep up with it all. And yet, it has also become a self-perpetuating priesthood, relentlessly expanding its own power and prestige.

There are two big problems with the explosive growth of HR. To start with, it has become a hub of corporate wokery, with whole departments staffed by social activists more interested in pushing a political agenda instead of developing new products or exploring new markets.

The reason so many organisations have gone all in on trans rights, or apologising for their “colonial past”, is not because anyone really wants them to. Most customers filling up their car with petrol or checking their bank account are perfectly happy if the company just delivers whatever product it sells efficiently, at a fair price, and couldn’t care less what it thinks about Palestine or whatever else is the fashionable cause of the day. It is foisted on the organisation by HR activists.

Next, it bogs a company down in processes, demanding constant meetings, and setting objectives that drain every ounce of energy from the working day. It takes the purpose and focus out of the organisation, and replaces it with a soul-sapping conformity.

Of course, we will have to see how Bolt gets on over the next couple of years. Perhaps it really will collapse as people leave, given that they can no longer bring their “whole self” to the office.

Time will tell. But here’s a bet. I expect it will thrive as it breaks free of the tyranny of box-ticking codes and goals. Without an HR department, its people will be more motivated, and it will have more space to innovate. 

Breslow has done businesses across the world a huge favour by breaking the biggest corporate taboo of the last decade. Just fire the “people professionals” – and let the company start to breathe again.

data.title: Britain’s only privately owned train is now for hire – if you’ve got £45,000 to spare
data.description:
Mussolini used his private train to showcase the fascist future and to meet Hitler in bombproof tunnels. Tsar Nicholas II’s lavishly appointed imperial train was used for Romanov family jaunts to St Petersburg and to inspect troops at the front during the First World War. Marshal Foch’s dining car, the Compiègne Wagon, on his personal train, was used to sign the armistice in 1918. British monarchs rode in royal carriages to fulfil the national “duty” of being seen by the masses – at least, until helicopters became available.

Kim Jong Un’s Taeyangho train, aka the “Moving Fortress”, reportedly has machine-gun emplacements, surface-to-air missiles and anti-tank guided missiles.

What would you use your private train for?

Soon, you’ll be able to find out. On May 18, The Chairman’s Train – owned by rail-mad multi-millionaire Jeremy Hosking – was unveiled at Euston. As well as bespoke tours, it will be the first train in the UK available for private hire in its entirety – that is, nine cars, all for yourself and a friend or 15. The cost of a ticket for this over-the-top travel indulgence? Final rates are yet to be released, but as a guide, the company’s spokesperson says: “exclusive hire of The Chairman’s Train would start from £45,000 for the first day, with subsequent days from £25,000 per day thereafter.”

It’s a lot of money, obviously, but given that L’Observatoire on Belmond’s Venice-Simplon Orient Express train starts at £80,000 per night – and it’s only a single carriage – you could describe The Chairman’s Train as competitive, within its minuscule market sector.

Now decked out in classic 1950s British Rail “blood and custard” livery, the coaches were previously used on Caledonian Sleeper services. Gutted and refitted by engineering firm Independent Rail, they feature all-new luxury interiors by designer Sara Oliver. Guest suites are finished with handcrafted marquetry – by the family firm that did the woodwork on Titanic – fine linens and walk‑in showers.

Upscale upholsterer George Smith produced bespoke sofas and footstools, while Robert Kime supplied additional furnishings and soothing lighting. Curtains were crafted by Guy Goodfellow. There are two bar cars, both with pianos, and a drawing room car with a cabinet of curiosities – inspired by the Duke of Urbino’s studiolo, designed as a place to study and reflect at the Palazzo Ducale di Gubbio. The top on-board pad, the Chairman’s Suite, is a double bedroom with its own dressing room and twin-basin bathroom, and the best views.

But everyone on the train has access to panoramic observation cars that offer sweeping views of the landscapes it passes through. A dedicated “bubble car” called Flora will be used to take guests on excursions using remote branch lines and into locations unreachable by standard services.

From this summer, the train – hauled by “a carefully selected combination of vintage steam, electric and diesel locomotives” – will offer tours for up to 16 passengers.

The inaugural season begins with the West Highland Experience from July 10-14, followed by a second tour from Aug 14-18. Departing from Euston, the train will travel north on the West Coast Main Line, with an overnight stop at Armathwaite in the Eden Valley. On the second day, the train continues to Fort William, where “Flora” will transport passengers directly from the train to their hotel for a two-night stay. The return journey travels south via the West Coast Main Line from Rannoch to Kilmarnock, where guests will stay overnight before returning to London Euston on the final day via Carlisle.

Anyone who has been on a posh train knows how much time there is for food and drink, notwithstanding the siestas. On The Chairman’s Train, seasonal menus “curated” (of course) by in-house chef Nicholas Allen will appear like magic at breakfast, lunch and dinner, with drinks and canapés for the quiet periods. During hotel stays, breakfast and dinner will be taken at the hotel, with lunch included at one of Scotland’s top-flight restaurants.

Prices for the five-day West Highland journey start from £9,000 per room and include all transfers, food, excursions and accommodation on and off the train.

From 2027, The Chairman’s Train offering will expand to include bespoke itineraries and exclusive private charters across the United Kingdom. Guests who can afford the hefty whole-train fee will be able to commission journeys tailored around specific interests, from castles, cathedrals and gardens to golf, coastal landscapes and heritage destinations.

Hosking already runs heritage trains through his Crewe-based venture Locomotive Services Limited, taking advantage of the UK’s open-access system, which allows firms willing to shoulder commercial risks to use the network. In 2022, it acquired Steam Dreams, which offers a range of holidays aboard trains pulled by grand old engines like Blue Peter and Britannia.

Rail fans love to spot these on the move and have already begun to log the trial runs of The Chairman’s Train. But who will be giving them the royal wave from the window once the private charters start? This tantalising detail remains to be seen.

Visit thechairmanstrain.com or call 020 7846 1200 for updates and booking.

data.title: Next boss attacks Labour over ‘dramatic fall’ in entry-level jobs
data.description:
The boss of Next has criticised Labour over a “dramatic fall” in entry-level jobs as retailers brace for a new ban on zero-hours contracts.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise blamed Rachel Reeves’s £25bn increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions for a “crisis in youth employment” that he warned would only get worse.

In two years, the number of applicants for every shop job at clothing retailer Next has soared from 10 to 19, he warned.

The Conservative peer told the BBC: “That doubling of applicants for shop jobs is indicative of just how big the crisis is in youth unemployment at the moment.”

Official figures show that the unemployment rate for 16 to 24-year-olds has hit a 12-year high of 16.2pc, more than triple the general unemployment rate of 5pc.

Lord Wolfson called on the Government to reverse the rise in employers’ National Insurance, announced by the Chancellor in her record tax-raising first Budget in 2024, which he said had driven a slump in entry-level jobs alongside larger-than-inflation minimum wage rises.

Lord Wolfson, who has previously said that government policies added £70m a year to Next’s wage bill, said cost increases meant the retailer had fewer staff in its shops.

He also warned that new rules under the Government’s Employment Rights Act – which was championed by Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister – would make hiring even more difficult for retailers.

The reforms will ban zero-hours contracts and force retailers to give staff a guaranteed minimum number of weekly hours.

Retailers have claimed this will prevent them from offering existing low-hour workers more shifts during busy periods, including Christmas, saying they would then need to give them those same hours throughout the year.

However, Labour has argued that its Employment Rights Act, which is being phased in from this year, will provide greater security for employees by ending contracts it says are “exploitative”.

The Act, which is the biggest overhaul of employment rights in decades, also gives workers a fleet of day-one rights and reduces the qualifying period for unfair dismissal from two years to six months.

‘Damaging economic growth’

Alice Martin, the head of research at The Work Foundation at Lancaster University, said the reforms were “long overdue”, adding: “One in five workers in the UK is in severely insecure work, without predictable pay or basic protections.”

She suggested retail chiefs would be wrong to claim the Employment Rights Act was fuelling Britain’s youth unemployment crisis, saying: “These reforms have not yet come into force, so blaming current hiring trends on them is misplaced.”

The share of business leaders who think the Employment Rights Act will hurt economic growth in the UK has climbed from 72pc last May to 86pc this month, now that the measures are coming into force, according to new research by the Institute of Directors (IoD).

Nearly two-thirds (63pc) said they were less likely to hire new staff as a result, while 57pc said they were more likely to invest in automation.

Alex Hall-Chen, the IoD’s principal policy adviser for employment, warned the new laws were a “strong deterrent to hiring” because they made it riskier and more expensive for employers to take on new staff.

She said: “These findings are a clear sign that the Employment Rights Act is damaging economic growth and job creation in the UK.

“Our research found that business leaders are already halting or scaling back plans to hire in the UK, outsourcing roles more aggressively and automating roles where possible in direct response to the reforms.”

Lord Wolfson said the changes came alongside a whole host of government policies which were “holding the economy back”.

UK GDP rose by 0.6pc in the first three months of this year – up from 0.2pc in the previous quarter – but economists have warned that this was partly a “statistical illusion”.

In April, the International Monetary Fund warned that the energy shock from the war in Iran would hit Britain harder than any other advanced economy as it cut its forecast for UK growth this year from 1.3pc to 0.8pc.

Lord Wolfson said: “Youth unemployment is really a symptom of wider problems with employment in the economy and of course, if you’ve got fewer jobs, the people who suffer most are the people with the least experience and that is the youngest.”

Companies have warned that higher taxes and wages have disproportionately hit entry-level jobs in hospitality and retail, which employ a larger proportion of people on minimum wage.

Lord Wolfson said that Next is increasingly using technology such as self-scanning lockers for returns, which means it can reduce the number of staff operating its tills.

A Treasury spokesman said that Ms Reeves’s minimum wage rises have increased pay for younger workers and that employers’ National Insurance contributions were smaller for under-21s.

“Cutting wages for the lowest paid during a time of global uncertainty is not the answer,” they said.

A business department spokesman said the Budget had allowed the Government to stabilise the economy and deliver support for families and businesses.

“Lord Wolfson, who earned more than £7m last year, will understand just how important our measures to make work pay are for the financial and job security of working people,” the spokesman added.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ousted BP chairman pulled into row over 5-star hotel stays
data.description:
The ousted chairman of BP has defended staying at an upmarket hotel in London amid an escalating row about his conduct.

A spokesman for Albert Manifold, who was sacked without warning on Tuesday, said he had stayed at the Haymarket hotel, in the capital’s West End, on approximately 13 occasions when attending company meetings.

The spokesman said the stays were necessitated by the fact that he “lives in Ireland”, adding that the hotel is “a short five-minute walk” from BP’s headquarters.

Suggestions that Mr Manifold’s stay at the five-star hotel had contradicted his criticism of “excessive” spending at BP were rejected.

“It is not Claridge’s,” the spokesman said. “It is not the Ritz.”

Mr Manifold’s abrupt dismissal from BP, announced in a short statement to the stock exchange, has descended quickly into a bitter war of words after the Irishman refused to accept “lies” about his conduct.

Claims by Dame Amanda Blanc, BP’s senior independent director, that the board had “serious concerns” about his behaviour were quickly followed by anonymous briefings accusing Mr Manifold of “bullying” both junior and senior members of staff.

However, the Irishman swiftly rejected the allegations.

In a statement, Mr Manifold acknowledged that he had “pushed hard and challenged people directly”.

However, he said there is “considerable distance between driving an organisation with urgency and the characterisation of my conduct that is now being put about”.

Mr Manifold added: “What I do not accept is that lies can be told about me, nor that anyone should be allowed to hide behind anonymity when commenting on my time at BP.

“At no point in my tenure as chairman of BP has anyone raised with me any issue about my conduct or my relationship with my colleagues.”

Among the reasons given for BP’s decision to dismiss Mr Manifold is that he clashed with Ben Mathews, the company secretary, over costs.

Mr Mathews had subsequently been a “driver” of BP’s decision to remove Mr Manifold, it has been reported.

Mr Mathews reported directly to Mr Manifold, and helped prepare for the company’s annual meeting, at which 18pc of BP shareholders voted against the chairman’s election at the AGM.

The ousting of Mr Manifold has left the company in disarray. In April, BP appointed Meg O’Neill as its fifth chief executive in six years, and it now faces having to find its third chairman in just two years.

BP’s share price is down 5pc since the news of Mr Manifold’s sacking was announced – equivalent to a £4.3bn hit to its stock market value.

A source close to Mr Manifold described the latest attempts to dent his reputation as “a joke”.

BP declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Royal Mail fails to deliver first-class post on time for nearly a decade
data.description:
Royal Mail has not met its delivery targets for first-class letters in almost a decade amid an escalating crisis at the postal service.

The company’s latest figures reveal that it delivered just 75.7pc of first-class mail within one working day in the year to March, meaning almost a quarter of letters arrived late.

That fell short of Royal Mail’s universal service obligation (USO), which requires it to deliver 93pc of first-class letters within a day.

Only 90.2pc of second-class post was delivered within three working days, which also fails to meet its 98.5pc target.

The figures mark a deterioration from the previous year and the latest failure for the postal service, which has not met its targets for first-class post since 2017. It has also been six years since it met its second-class targets.

Overall, the postal service is estimated to have delivered 219 million letters late this year.

Tom MacInnes, of Citizens Advice, branded Royal Mail’s track record “completely unacceptable”, adding: “For the company, poor performance is business as usual.”

Missing the targets will trigger an investigation by Ofcom that could lead to yet another fine for the postal service. The regulator handed down a £21m fine for late deliveries last year, adding to a string of recent penalties.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “We are very concerned to see the latest delivery figures from Royal Mail today and will announce next steps shortly.”

The Telegraph understands Ofcom is planning to launch an investigation early next week.

Royal Mail is under fierce political scrutiny over its poor quality of service, with MPs branding it a “national institution in meltdown”.

This has been compounded by continued increases in stamp prices, with the price of a first-class stamp hitting £1.80 in April – almost double what it cost in 2022.

The company, which was bought by Daniel Křetínský, a Czech billionaire, in a £3.6bn deal last year, has vowed to invest £500m over the next five years to improve its quality of service.

But it has argued that the improvement plan is contingent on sweeping reforms to the USO, including scrapping second-class letter deliveries on Saturdays.

Royal Mail was locked in a lengthy dispute with the Communication Workers Union (CWU) over the reforms, but the two sides have reached an agreement.

The result of a ballot at the CWU, which represents 100,000 postal workers, is due on Friday afternoon. If approved, the changes are expected to be rolled out across the country before the peak Christmas period.

Ofcom is also under fire for its handling of the Royal Mail’s poor performance, with MPs warning that the regulator was “not up to the job” of regulating an increasingly complex and competitive market.

The watchdog insisted it had acted “decisively, using the full extent of our powers, to modernise Royal Mail’s regulatory obligations”.

Targets have been lowered

Royal Mail said its quality of service was already improving thanks to measures including targeted support for poorly-performing delivery offices and additional investment to tackle sick absences.

By the end of March, the postal service was delivering 81.1pc of first-class mail within one working day and 90.2pc of second-class post within three working days.

As part of the reforms, Royal Mail’s delivery targets have been watered down. As of April, only 90pc of first-class letters must be delivered the next day, while 99pc must arrive within three working days. For second-class post, 95pc must be delivered within three days and 99pc within five days.

MPs have also pointed the finger at Amazon, accusing the US tech giant of “hiving off” profits from Royal Mail.

In a report published last week, the Commons business and trade committee raised concerns that the US tech giant is benefitting from Royal Mail’s nationwide delivery network without contributing to the huge costs of maintaining its infrastructure.

Amazon did not respond to a request for comment.

Jamie Stephenson, Royal Mail’s chief operating officer, said: “We’re putting significant investment into improving reliability and reaching these new delivery targets, but delivering lasting change across a network of this scale takes time.”

He added that the new delivery model would be deployed by Christmas, and that early performance this year indicated the company was moving in the right direction.

data.title: Thousands blocked from bank compensation under Labour shake-up
data.description:
Thousands of people will be blocked from making claims against banks and insurers for historic wrongdoing under plans for a 10-year limit on complaints.

Around 3,000 people a year receive compensation for mistreatment that took place a decade or more ago, receiving an average of £200 each in redress.

But they face losing the ability to make claims to the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) thanks to a package of measures proposed by the Treasury to save businesses more than £125m per year.

It comes after the Payment Protection Insurance (PPI) mis-selling scandal, which resulted in banks paying more than £36bn in compensation to customers.

The scandal triggered a series of attempts by lenders to impose deadlines on claims as they sought to draw a line under the matter. PPI had humiliated the nation’s biggest lenders, further wrecked their reputations and hampered efforts to rebuild their balance sheets after the financial crisis.

Under the Financial Services and Markets Bill, the Government hopes to streamline the rules and reduce the number of regulators each company has to deal with, to reduce the administrative burden on the City.

Financial companies pay around £18m of fees each year to the FOS to investigate claims for old financial products, most of which are rejected each year.

Removing the ability to make historic claims should also free up resources to allow the regulator to more rapidly evaluate complaints about more recent contracts with customers.

‘Strengthen consumer protections’

UK Finance said the time limit would help return the ombudsman “to its original purpose as a fast and impartial dispute resolution body”.

“Throughout the consultation process on FOS reform, we supported the introduction of a time limit for bringing complaints as it will provide greater certainty and help reduce administrative burdens,” the industry group said.

However, the consumer group Which? criticised the changes. Rocío Concha, its director of policy and advocacy, said: “The Financial Ombudsman Service is a vital safety net for consumers when something goes wrong, but the Government’s own impact assessment seems to back up our concerns that this bill could weaken existing protections.

“At a time when the cost of living crisis is pushing household budgets to breaking point, the Government should be looking to strengthen consumer protections against financial losses, rather than dilute them.”

Banks and other finance companies are also hoping to benefit from a change to guidance given to the FOS, stating that companies will be found to have behaved “fairly and reasonably” as long as they have followed the Financial Conduct Authority’s (FCA) rules.

Other changes include moving responsibility for anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing from 22 different regulators to the FCA and consolidating the Payment Systems Regulator into the FCA.

The Government said it was part of a move to cut the number of regulators, which boomed after the financial crisis and has become increasingly complicated in the past 20 years.

Similarly, the Senior Managers’ Regime, brought in to govern executives’ behaviour after a series of scandals, is being slimmed down. 

The aim is to halve the cost of the regime, speeding up hiring decisions, cutting the burden on small banks and focusing regulators and companies on genuinely risky behaviour instead of across-the-board regulations.
data.author: The telegraph
